EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through a warm and unstable airmass across Essex county New York during the afternoon of the 30th. Scattered thunderstorms developed across the region with some storms that produced large hail as well as damaging winds. Baseball sized hail was reported in Paradox as well as damaging winds that knocked down several trees. Golf ball sized hail was reported in Moriah with quarter size hail reported in Port Henry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball sized hail was reported in the town of Paradox as well as several downed trees.

As high pressure remained anchored off the mid-Atlantic Coast, a warm, humid southerly flow prevailed across the region. An approaching cold front triggered afternoon and evening thunderstorms. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines throughout numerous towns throughout the area. Across the western sections, these included Concord, Hamburg, Gerry, Westfield, Niagara Falls, LeRoy, Varysburg, Bennington, Otto, Orchard Park, Henrietta, Carlton, York, Bemus Point, Byron, Castile, Mount Morris, and Canaseraga. Further east, damage was reported in Manchester, Newark, Cato, Wolcott, Oswego, Carthage and Denmark. Utility companies reported numerous power outages throughout the area with upwards of 20,000 customers without power. In Rige, a house fire was blamed on a lightning strike from the storms.

As low pressure moved east from the western Great lakes, the remains of Tropical Storm Barry moved northeast from the Carolinas. Severe thunderstorms developed along a lake breeze over Niagara County. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in North Tonawanda and Lockport. As the localized storms crossed the Finger Lakes region, several inches of rain fell in a short amount of time washing out roads and driveways. In South Bristol, Mosher Road was washed away between County Road 33 and Gulick Road. In Honeoye, several driveways were washed out.

An arctic airmass moved into east central New York State late Thursday night on January 25th, and remained in place into Friday, January 26th. Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and ten degrees below zero, with some temperatures as low as 15 degrees below zero across higher elevations of the Adirondacks.|In addition, northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph produced wind chills as low as 25 to 30 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into east central New York State late Thursday night on January 25th, and remained in place into Friday, January 26th. Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and ten degrees below zero. |The coldest temperature recorded in eastern Rensselaer county was 7 degrees below zero at Stephentown.|In addition, northwest winds of 5 to 10 mph produced wind chills as low as 10 to 20 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed east across east central New York and western New England during the afternoon hours of Tuesday June 5th. The interaction of this strong front, combined with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Clifton Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the warmest six month period in history, lake effect season got off to a quick start about a week earlier than normal.  A sharp cold front moved across the region during Monday evening the 5th and lake plumes developed off Lake Erie prior to dawn of the 6th. Several inches fell by mid-morning across Chautauqua and northwest Cattaraugus counties. There were some power outages reported as the trees were still full-leafed and the weight of the snow snapped limbs.  An approaching upper level trough broke up the band as it lifted north of the area. Total snowfall accumulations of eight inches were reported from Mayville, South Dayton and Ellicottville.

A weak area of low pressure moved east from the Great Lakes to the New Jersey coast. This system combined with an upper level disturbance to bring a light accumulation of snow to the Southern Tier of New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the county. Center Lisle measured 4.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Ohio Valley to Chesapeake Bay and brought snow to much of New York State. Across portions of the Southern Tier and Finger Lakes Region, a blanket of eight to ten inches of snow fell including Scottsburg, Wayland, South Dayton and Angelica, for example.

Low pressure developed over the Tennessee Valley and deepened as it moved to northern Ohio then east to the New England Coast. The system spread heavy snows across the entire region from late on the 15th through much of the 16th. Many schools throughout the area canceled classes on the 16th. Numerous automobile accidents were blamed on the snow-covered, slippery roads. An upper level disturbance moved across northern New York and helped initiate the development of a weak surface low which moved along a frontal boundary across New York during the night of December 11th and into the early morning hours of the 12th. A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain overspread northern New York during the evening hours of the 11th but changed to accumulating snow during the early morning hours of the 12th before ending by mid-morning. Combined snow and sleet accumulations across northern New York were 3 to 6 inches. Some specific accumulations included; 6 inches in Malone (Franklin county), 5 inches in Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county), Tupper Lake (Franklin county) and 4 inches in Canton (St. Lawrence county), Newcomb (Essex county) and Plattsburgh (Clinton county).

As a low pressure system moved northeast across Southeast Canada, a high pressure system built southeast across the Ohio Valley.  This resulted in a strong northwest pressure gradient that caused winds to gust to 47 mph at JFK Airport and to 46 mph at Westchester County Airport and in Lindenhurst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds resulted in 1 injury in Midtown Manhattan.  Metal blew off a construction scaffold and injured a pedestrian near the intersection of 1st Avenue and East 82nd Street.  Glass blew off a building under construction near the intersection of West 40th and 8th Avenues.  A 20 by 10 foot billboard blew down into a parking lot near Remsen and Ave A in Brooklyn.

A deep low moved into the Great Lakes Region resulting in a dramatic warm-up into the 50s and over an inch of soaking rain. This unleashed snowmelt of the 4 to 7 inch snowpack, with lower elevations of the Niagara Frontier bare by late in the day. Rivers and creeks swelled with three Buffalo area creeks (Cazenovia, Buffalo and Cayuga) going over Flood Stage during the evening of the 23rd. Cazenovia Creek at Ebenezer (Flood Stage 10 feet) crested at 10.8 feet. Cayuga Creek at Lancaster (Flood Stage 8 feet) crested at 8.4 feet. Buffalo Creek at Gardenville (Flood Stage 7 feet) crested at 7.3 feet. Only minor flooding was reported and no significant damage occurred.

A potent cold front crossed the region during the afternoon hours. Behind the front, windy conditions developed. Scattered power outages were reported across the western parts of the Niagara Frontier and the strong winds were funneled over Lake Erie. Sustained winds to 40 mph were measured at Niagara Falls International Airport and gusts to 61 mph were measured at Buffalo Niagara International Airport.

The first true arctic air mass of the season swept into western and central New York on the heels of a major synoptic storm during the first few days of December. A strong west to northwest flow followed the storm early on the third and changed to rain to snow from west to east during the morning hours. The snow was heavy at times across higher elevations south of Buffalo during the afternoon and evening where several inches fell. Very strong northwest winds brought intense streamers in from Lake Huron across Lake Erie. Off Lake Ontario, multiple band streamers developed by late Monday (the third) which continued through most of Tuesday.  The activity wound down late Tuesday, the fourth.  Specific snowfall reports off Lake Erie included: |Warsaw 15���; Friendship 13���, Olean 11���; Perrysburg and Franklinville 10���; and Jamestown 7���. An upper level disturbance moved across northern New York and helped initiate the development of a weak surface low which moved along a frontal boundary across New York during the night of December 11th and into the early morning hours of the 12th. A wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain overspread northern New York during the evening hours of the 11th but changed to accumulating snow during the early morning hours of the 12th before ending by mid-morning. Combined snow and sleet accumulations across northern New York were 3 to 6 inches. Some specific accumulations included; 6 inches in Malone (Franklin county), 5 inches in Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county), Tupper Lake (Franklin county) and 4 inches in Canton (St. Lawrence county), Newcomb (Essex county) and Plattsburgh (Clinton county).

A potent cold front crossed the region during the afternoon hours. Behind the front, windy conditions developed. Scattered power outages were reported across the western parts of the Niagara Frontier and the strong winds were funneled over Lake Erie. Sustained winds to 40 mph were measured at Niagara Falls International Airport and gusts to 61 mph were measured at Buffalo Niagara International Airport.

An upper level disturbance moved across northern New York during the early morning hours of December 31st. In addition, a surface low moved north along the mid-Atlantic coast on the night of the 30th to southeast of Cape Cod by daybreak on the 31st.||Snow overspread northern New York before Midnight on the 31st and ended by daybreak on the 31st, with snowfall amounts 3 to 6 inches across the region. Some specific snowfall totals included; 6 inches in Peru (Clinton county) and Newcomb (Essex county), 5 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin county) with 4 inches in Canton (St. Lawrence county), Ellenburg Depot and Plattsburgh (Clinton county).

A deep low moved into the Great Lakes Region resulting in a dramatic warm-up into the 50s and over an inch of soaking rain. This unleashed snowmelt of the 4 to 7 inch snowpack, with lower elevations of the Niagara Frontier bare by late in the day. Rivers and creeks swelled with three Buffalo area creeks (Cazenovia, Buffalo and Cayuga) going over Flood Stage during the evening of the 23rd. Cazenovia Creek at Ebenezer (Flood Stage 10 feet) crested at 10.8 feet. Cayuga Creek at Lancaster (Flood Stage 8 feet) crested at 8.4 feet. Buffalo Creek at Gardenville (Flood Stage 7 feet) crested at 7.3 feet. Only minor flooding was reported and no significant damage occurred.

Low pressure centered over southern Indiana tracked northeast and spread freezing rain across the area. Up to a half-inch of ice accumulated on trees and wires. The weight brought down some power lines with outages scattered throughout the area.  Heavy icing was reported in Fulton, Warsaw, Livonia, Geneva, Henderson and North Osceola.

A strong westerly flow reignited lake effect snows off both Lakes Erie and Ontario Wednesday morning (31st). The activity was very intense off Lake Ontario, focusing near the Oswego-Jefferson County line and extending right across the Tug Hill. Snow fell at 2 to 4 inches per hour and piled up to 1 to 3 feet on the southwestern portion of the plateau. The activity broke up a bit and lifted north in the evening as shear increased. Off Lake Erie, a broader area of disorganized lake snow prevailed through most of the morning before it consolidated into an intense west to east band over Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties at midday. This band lifted north across Buffalo's south Towns by late afternoon, but dissipated right as it reached Buffalo due to increasing southerly shear. The movement of the band precluded heavy accumulations with most areas only seeing heavy snow for an hour or two, with totals generally 4 to 6 inches with a few outliers of up to 8 on the Chautauqua ridge and over to Ellicottville. |Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall reports included: 35 inches at Redfield, 16 inches at Mannsville, 15 inches as North Osceola, 13 inches as Hooker, and 12 inches at Highmarket.

January's second lake effect event followed closely on the heels of a sharp cold front which crossed the region early on Friday 19th. A moist westerly flow was primed and even though it was only marginally cold enough, the moisture in lower levels was enough to set off the activity, which primarily focused on Oswego county for much of Friday midday and afternoon before settling southward as winds veered. Rates up to two inches per hour were noted Friday afternoon along with several reports of thunder. The Lake Erie activity was more spread out and multiple banded but did pile up on some of the ridges and ski areas. Total amounts were as much as 12-18 over much of Oswego County.|Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included: 12 inches at Ellicottville and 10 inches at South Dayton.  Off Lake Ontario, snowfall reports included: 17 inches at Constantia, 14 inches at Fulton, 13 inches at West Monroe and 9 inches as Parish.

January's third lake effect event was associated with the passage of a cold front during the afternoon and early evening of Tuesday (23rd). Off Lake Ontario, several inches of snow fell over Watertown in the southwest flow ahead of the front. The locally heavy snow bands moved over the Tug Hill later in the evening as the flow turned westerly. Some thunder was even reported around midnight. The activity settled into Oswego County and dropped several inches Wednesday morning before cutting off by midday. Reported snowfall totals were in the eight to ten inch range, but radar indications are that a bit more fell in isolated areas.

True winter continued to deepen across the region during the last week of the month and with the lakes still open and warmer than usual due to the earlier mild winter.  A northwest flow developed Monday morning the 29th and set up an intense narrow band off Lake Ontario which focused on the three-county border of Wayne-Cayuga-Oswego. This band moved little for 4 to 6 hours, and dropped snow at the rate of up to 5 inches per hour! Twenty to thirty inches fell in a ten-mile wide area centered on Fair Haven and Hannibal. The flow backed a bit later in the day and sent the band north across central Oswego county with up to 6 inches falling before settling south again after midnight and dissipating. |Off Lake Erie, heavy lake snow developed across Chautauqua county in the early afternoon, and then slowly but steadily drifted north as the flow backed ahead of an approaching low. The band moved north across Buffalo's southtowns in the evening and dropped 5 to 10 inches of snow, but broke apart just as it reached the City of Buffalo. Upward of two feet fell during the day over the Chautauqua Ridge, and up to a foot south of Buffalo. |Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall reports included:  30 inches at Hannibal, 24 inches at Red Creek, 21 inches at Fair Haven, and 8 inches at West Monroe and Constantia.|Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included:  24 inches at Perrysburg, 23 inches at Arkwright, 18 inches as Cassadaga, 15 inches at Mayville, 10 inches at Hamburg and 9 inches at Orchard Park.

A significant surface low traveled along an arctic cold front that moved across northern New York during the night of the 2nd. This clipper system delivered snow to northern New York late in the afternoon and tapered off to snow showers before midnight. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches with the heaviest amounts in the Adirondacks.

A surge of seasonably cold air overspread the Lower Lakes region during the second week of January. Lake snows off Lake Erie began late Monday evening (8th) in a northwest flow following a frontal passage, with a bit of upslope component Monday night, then a narrow band of intense snow moved in off Lake Erie after midnight and focused on southern Erie, Wyoming, and the northern portions of Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties through mid morning Tuesday ahead of a weak trough. Up to a foot fell in a narrow band over these areas. Off Lake Ontario, a similar band developed over the Tug Hill Plateau. The activity broke apart and spread out a bit later Tuesday and Tuesday Night as flow turned northwest following the trough with some subsidence, then intensified a bit again off Ontario later Tuesday night and early Wednesday. Total amounts were as much as 16-19 in heaviest areas, but dropped off substantially elsewhere. |Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included:  18 inches at Perrsyburg, 16 inches at Ellicottville, 15 inches at South Dayton, 13 inches at Arcade, 10 inches at Warsaw, and 8 inches at Jamestown and Dunkirk. Off Lake Ontario, snowfall reports included: 19 inches at Highmarket, 17 inches at North Osceola, 8 inches at Walworth, and 7 inches in West Monroe.

A moist westerly flow was primed and even though it was only marginally cold enough, the moisture in lower levels was enough to set off the activity, which primarily focused on Oswego county for much of Friday midday and afternoon before settling southward as winds veered. Rates up to two inches per hour were noted Friday afternoon along with several reports of thunder. The Lake Erie activity was more spread out and multiple banded but did pile up on some of the ridges and ski areas. Total amounts were as much as 12-18 over much of Oswego County.|Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included: 12 inches at Ellicottville and 10 inches at South Dayton.  Off Lake Ontario, snowfall reports included: 17 inches at Constantia, 14 inches at Fulton, 13 inches at West Monroe and 9 inches as Parish.

Lake effect events continued to come fast and furious in the cold pattern of January.  As cold air deepened following the passage of an upper trough,  a narrow band of heavier snow developed right along the lakeshore of Monroe county and extended southeast into western Wayne County. This dropped 6 to 10 inches in just a few hours from the lakeshore of Irondequoit southeast across Penfield, Webster and Pittsford. There was a sharp cut off as the City of Rochester received only an inch or two.

True winter continued to deepen across the region during the last week of the month and with the lakes still open and warmer than usual due to the earlier mild winter.  A northwest flow developed Monday morning the 29th and set up an intense narrow band off Lake Ontario which focused on the three-county border of Wayne-Cayuga-Oswego. This band moved little for 4 to 6 hours, and dropped snow at the rate of up to 5 inches per hour! Twenty to thirty inches fell in a ten-mile wide area centered on Fair Haven and Hannibal. The flow backed a bit later in the day and sent the band north across central Oswego county with up to 6 inches falling before settling south again after midnight and dissipating. |Off Lake Erie, heavy lake snow developed across Chautauqua county in the early afternoon, and then slowly but steadily drifted north as the flow backed ahead of an approaching low. The band moved north across Buffalo's southtowns in the evening and dropped 5 to 10 inches of snow, but broke apart just as it reached the City of Buffalo. Upward of two feet fell during the day over the Chautauqua Ridge, and up to a foot south of Buffalo. |Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall reports included:  30 inches at Hannibal, 24 inches at Red Creek, 21 inches at Fair Haven, and 8 inches at West Monroe and Constantia.|Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included:  24 inches at Perrysburg, 23 inches at Arkwright, 18 inches as Cassadaga, 15 inches at Mayville, 10 inches at Hamburg and 9 inches at Orchard Park.

A strong westerly flow reignited lake effect snows off both Lakes Erie and Ontario Wednesday morning (31st). The activity was very intense off Lake Ontario, focusing near the Oswego-Jefferson County line and extending right across the Tug Hill. Snow fell at 2 to 4 inches per hour and piled up to 1 to 3 feet on the southwestern portion of the plateau. The activity broke up a bit and lifted north in the evening as shear increased. Off Lake Erie, a broader area of disorganized lake snow prevailed through most of the morning before it consolidated into an intense west to east band over Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties at midday. This band lifted north across Buffalo's south Towns by late afternoon, but dissipated right as it reached Buffalo due to increasing southerly shear. The movement of the band precluded heavy accumulations with most areas only seeing heavy snow for an hour or two, with totals generally 4 to 6 inches with a few outliers of up to 8 on the Chautauqua ridge and over to Ellicottville. |Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall reports included: 35 inches at Redfield, 16 inches at Mannsville, 15 inches as North Osceola, 13 inches as Hooker, and 12 inches at Highmarket.

A surge of seasonably cold air overspread the Lower Lakes region during the second week of January. Lake snows off Lake Erie began late Monday evening (8th) in a northwest flow following a frontal passage, with a bit of upslope component Monday night, then a narrow band of intense snow moved in off Lake Erie after midnight and focused on southern Erie, Wyoming, and the northern portions of Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties through mid morning Tuesday ahead of a weak trough. Up to a foot fell in a narrow band over these areas. Off Lake Ontario, a similar band developed over the Tug Hill Plateau. The activity broke apart and spread out a bit later Tuesday and Tuesday Night as flow turned northwest following the trough with some subsidence, then intensified a bit again off Ontario later Tuesday night and early Wednesday. Total amounts were as much as 16-19 in heaviest areas, but dropped off substantially elsewhere. |Off Lake Erie, specific snowfall reports included:  18 inches at Perrsyburg, 16 inches at Ellicottville, 15 inches at South Dayton, 13 inches at Arcade, 10 inches at Warsaw, and 8 inches at Jamestown and Dunkirk. Off Lake Ontario, snowfall reports included: 19 inches at Highmarket, 17 inches at North Osceola, 8 inches at Walworth, and 7 inches in West Monroe.

Low pressure centered over southern Indiana tracked northeast and spread freezing rain across the area. Up to a half-inch of ice accumulated on trees and wires. The weight brought down some power lines with outages scattered throughout the area.  Heavy icing was reported in Fulton, Warsaw, Livonia, Geneva, Henderson and North Osceola.

Low pressure off the Delmarva Peninsula moved northeast at deepened off Cape Cod. The large low pressure area brought a blanket of heavy snow across the entire region. The snow began during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 16th and continued through the late night hours of Saturday night. A general eight to ten inches of snow fell across the area with slightly higher amounts downwind of Lakes Erie and Ontario. The heavy snow, which fell on the heels of significant flooding across the western sections, brought a return to mid-winter conditions and was blamed for a rash of automobile accidents.

The Friday night-Saturday morning period saw an intense band across southern Jefferson county with somewhat of a repeat along the south shore of the Lake Saturday night and Sunday morning. Conditions finally moderated enough to shut down any lake snows late Sunday.|     Some representative total amounts off Lake Erie: Perrysburg 26���; Mayville 23���; Arkwright 18���; and Jamestown 15���. Lake Effect snowbands, combined with gusty winds produced near whiteout conditions at times from Monday morning, into Monday evening on March 5th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the region. |As a result of the poor visibilities and slippery road conditions, a seven vehicle crash occurred on the New York State Thruway, approximately one mile west of exit 29 in Canajoharie. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Effect snowbands, combined with gusty winds produced near whiteout conditions at times from Monday morning, into Monday evening on March 5th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the region. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Delmarva Peninsula moved northeast at deepened off Cape Cod. The large low pressure area brought a blanket of heavy snow across the entire region. The snow began during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 16th and continued through the late night hours of Saturday night. A general eight to ten inches of snow fell across the area with slightly higher amounts downwind of Lakes Erie and Ontario. The heavy snow, which fell on the heels of significant flooding across the western sections, brought a return to mid-winter conditions and was blamed for a rash of automobile accidents.

Warmer air advected into the region on Tuesday March 13th into Wednesday 14th. This led to significant snowmelt across much of east central New York, particularly across the western Mohawk valley and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice began to melt and move along many streams, creeks and rivers during this time.|A slow moving cold front then dropped southeast across the region late Wednesday into Thursday, accompanied by moderate to locally heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, with higher amounts of up to, or slightly in excess of one inch across the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks.|The combination of rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding of small streams and creeks across portions of the east central New York State, starting on March 14th, and lingering until March 18th. In addition, localized river flooding also occurred, especially where ice jams developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam moving along the Mohawk River near the Northway Bridge resulted in flooding between Halfmoon and Clifton Park.  The flooding led to several road closures, including Riverview Road between Boyack Road and Van Vranken Road.

Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the region. |As a result of the poor visibilities and slippery road conditions, a seven vehicle crash occurred on the New York State Thruway, approximately one mile west of exit 29 in Canajoharie. The accident occurred around 4:20 PM EST, and involved three tractor trailers, three cars and one pickup truck. |There were 2 fatalities as a result of the crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Effect snowbands, combined with gusty winds produced near whiteout conditions at times from Monday morning, into Monday evening on March 5th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches across Hamilton county.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Hurricane Noel passed 200 miles to the Southeast of Montauk Point, as they were beginning to develop into a potent extra-tropical cyclone. They spread winds of 40-45 mph with gusts to 50-55 mph, knocking down trees and power lines across southern portions of Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and utility poles knocked down across the Hamptons by estimated 50 kt wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front produced showers and thunderstorms ahead of it, producing heavy rainfall across New York City. Rainfall amounts ranged around 1 inch, producing areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on West Side Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and injured 3 men and 1 boy after they pulled off on the side of the road and got off their motorcycles.  The 3 men were 32, 25, and 40 years old.  The boy was 13, who required CPR.  All 4 people were taken to Bon Secours Community Hospital in Port Jervis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This relatively modest lake effect event followed closely behind a widespread regional snowfall. A northwest flow followed this storm and set up some narrow but intense bands from far northeast Wayne County into Oswego County. The activity weakened and sheared out by mid morning of 16th as wind fields became unfavorable. The event was brief and localized, but did drop some heavy snow. Specific reports included: Palermo 16���; Fair Haven 15���; and Hannibal 14���.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure traveled east along a nearly stationary front during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday May 16th. This triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York State into western New England. Some thunderstorms became locally severe. The low pressure system, and attendant cold front and thunderstorms, moved southeast of the region by Wednesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down from thunderstorm winds in Ancram. Roof damage was also reported as a result of a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern New York State during the afternoon hours of Friday May 25th. This triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms, a few of which became strong to severe during the early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated moderate coastal flooding lingered behind a strong Nor'easter that impacted coastal New England in the days prior to Wednesday, April 18. The combination of a persistent long fetch northeast flow and a period of higher than normal spring tides resulted in tidal piling in the back bays of western Long Island.

A cold front located over the Great Lakes approached the region from the west late Monday, July 9th. A prefrontal trough well ahead of this front, in conjunction with a lake breeze boundary, and a hot, humid and unstable air mass in place, led to the rapid development of scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York State and western New England during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Monday.||In addition, some thunderstorms contained locally intense rainfall rates of 2 or more inches per hour.  This led to localized flash flooding in urbanized areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in North Greenbush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system moved across Ontario and Quebec on the 4th with a frontal boundary that moved across northern New York during the afternoon. Rain mixed with and then changed to sleet and snow across northern New York during the afternoon. Combined snow and sleet accumulations ranged from a slushy inch in the St. Lawrence River Valley to up to 6 inches in isolated locations in the Adirondacks. This caused some treacherous travel for the late afternoon and evening commute with several motor vehicle accidents reported.|Some specific snowfall accumulations included; 2 inches in Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county) and Newcomb (Essex county), 3 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin county) and 6 inches in Dannemora (Clinton county).

Warm temperatures from April 19th to April 24th led to rapid melting of a deep snowpack across much of the Adirondacks.  Antecendent water equivalent in the snow pack ranged from 6 to 16 inches.  This rapid melting led to flooding of many rivers that drain the Adirondacks. In addition, releases from Sacandaga Lake perpetuated the flooding on some rivers through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowmelt led to moderate flooding of the Hudson River at Schuylerville. The river crested at 92.8 feet at 0600 EST on April 25th, and remained above the flood stage of 90 feet through April 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowmelt led to moderate flooding of the Schroon River between Schroon Lake and the Hudson River.  At Riverbank on the Schroon River, the water exceeded the flood stage of 7 feet at 1700 EST on April 20th, crested at 8.92 feet at 0545 EST on April 25th, and fell back below flood stage at 2130 EST on April 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours ahead of an approaching cold front. In the City of Jamestown, the strong thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires.

Strong low pressure moved into the Great Lakes on the morning of the 12th. Meanwhile, a secondary area of low pressure developed off the Delmarva pennisula during the afternoon of the 12th. Both systems eventually moved into the Gulf of Maine during the early morning hours of the 13th.|Precipitation was a wintry mix of heavy wet snow, sleet and rain with the greatest snowfall totals occurring in the higher elevations. Snowfall was generally 5 to 7 inches in the slightly higher elevations of the Adirondacks with lesser amounts in the lower valleys. Some specific snowfall totals include; 7 inches in Dannemora (Clinton county) and Minerva (Essex county) with 6 inches in Saranac (Clinton county), Moriah and Newcomb (Essex county).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system, accompanied by a cold front moved across northern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. Surface instability was marginal but there was a relatively strong low level wind field. Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across northern New York during the mid afternoon to evening hours with isolated reports of wind damage. Trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Fine (St. Lawrence county) and Morrisonville (Clinton county), with some minor structural damage in Morrisville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down along Route 3 in Fine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed over Oswego county and produced damaging winds during the afternoon hours. Winds, estimated to be gusting near 60 mph, downed trees and power lines.

A weak cold front crossed the area triggering isolated severe thunderstorms over parts of the western southern tier and easter Lake Ontario region. Trees were downed in Hinsdale. Trees and wires were reported down in Leyden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern New York during the late afternoon and evening hours on Saturday, September 8th. This front, combined with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of scattered thunderstorms across eastern New York, some of which became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A southbound train that left Poughkeepsie struck a tree that had fallen due to strong thunderstorm winds on the tracks north of the New Hamburg station.||No injuries were reported, but an air hose on the train became detached.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary was located across southeast New York and southwest New England during Sunday September 9th. A wave of low pressure then developed over the southern Great Lakes, and traveled east along this boundary, leading to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms during Sunday afternoon across portions of eastern New York and western New England, a few of which became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were reported down throughout the town of Middleburgh due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moderately unstable airmass across the Champlain Valley of New York during the afternoon of the 16th. One thunderstorm developed in a favorable, highly sheared wind environment which eventually intensified into a supercell thunderstorm across Clinton county, NY. This supercell thunderstorm moved across the New York locations of Jericho, Keesville, northern Plattsburgh and Cumberlandhead, before heading into Lake Champlain and eventually Vermont. Significant straight-line wind damage (estimated between 60 and 80 mph) in the form of snapped, uprooted and downed trees, downed power lines and structural damage such as trees on houses as well as roof shingles and siding blown off. Also, several boats and docks were damaged in local marina. Large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large plate glass window blown in at Robert Moses Power Dam administrative building. Also, trees down in Robert Moses State park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southeast from the Great Lakes through central New York and northeast Pennsylvania during the evening. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the boundary during the afternoon and evening hours. There were numerous reports of large hail and wind damage from these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inch diameter trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the eastern Great Lakes Wednesday evening, May 9th, tracked east into Upstate New York by Thursday morning on the 10th. The low pressure system dragged a cold front through the region on the 10th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of the cold front, which swept through the region by late evening on the 10th. The thunderstorms produced mainly large hail, with a couple reports of wind damage and a small tornado.

Low pressure over the eastern Great Lakes Wednesday evening, May 9th, tracked east into Upstate New York by Thursday morning on the 10th. The low pressure system dragged a cold front through the region on the 10th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon ahead of the cold front, which swept through the region by late evening on the 10th. The thunderstorms produced mainly large hail, with a couple reports of wind damage and a small tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front that moved across Upstate New York late in the afternoon. Several of the storms produced strong, damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced penny size hail in Norwich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Northeast United States, bringing abundant sunshine and unusually warm temperatures. High temperature records were broken for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperature records were broken on several days at the Binghamton Regional Airport. On October 5th, the high reached 78, breaking the record of 77 set in 1951. On October 6th, the high reached 82, breaking the old record of 77 degrees set in 1990. A record was also set on the 9th, when the high reached 79, breaking the previous record of 78 in 1959. Normal high temperatures for this time of year are about 60 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Northeast United States, bringing abundant sunshine and unusually warm temperatures. High temperature records were broken for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperature records were tied or broken on several days at the Syracuse airport. On October 5th, the high reached 85, tying the record set in 1951. On October 6th, the high reached 85, breaking the old record of 84 degrees set in 1990. On October 8th, the high temperature of 80 degrees tied the old record of 80 set in 1993. Normal high temperatures for this time of year in Syracuse are about 64 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moderately unstable airmass across the Champlain Valley of New York during the afternoon of the 16th. One thunderstorm developed in a favorable, highly sheared wind environment which eventually intensified into a supercell thunderstorm across Clinton county, NY. This supercell thunderstorm moved across the New York locations of Jericho, Keesville, northern Plattsburgh and Cumberlandhead, before heading into Lake Champlain and eventually Vermont. Significant straight-line wind damage (estimated between 60 and 80 mph) in the form of snapped, uprooted and downed trees, downed power lines and structural damage such as trees on houses as well as roof shingles and siding blown off. Also, several boats and docks were damaged in local marina. Large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of large hail ranging from Quarter size to Golf ball size. Large hail resulted in several broken car windshields, dented vehicles and a few house windows broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid and unstable airmass, interacted with a lake breeze boundary and led to numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the late afternoon hours of Wednesday June 27th, some of which were severe.||In addition, locally very heavy rain led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixth Avenue was closed due to deep standing water from torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms accompanied the passage of a cold front across the region during the evening and early overnight hours. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Sanborn, several homes sustained minor damage to roofs and siding. In Mendon, a tree fell on a home. In Orchard Park, a 15- to 20-foot section of the Buffalo Bills Field house was torn off by the thunderstorm winds.

A surface low moved across northern New York during the morning of December 3rd. Snow overspread the region during the afternoon of December 2nd and changed to a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the early morning hours of the 3rd before gradually ending by midday as scattered snow showers. ||Snowfall amounts were 6 to 12 inches across the northern St. Lawrence River Valley and northern slopes of the northern Adirondacks of northern New York, where there was less of a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. Meanwhile, 3 to 6 inches of snow along with ice accumulations up to 1/4 of an inch occurred across the southern St. Lawrence River Valley and the Adirondacks of southeast St. Lawrence and southern Franklin counties. Some of the higher snowfall totals included; 12 inches in Potsdam (St. Lawrence county) and Bombay (Franklin county), 10 inches in Norfolk and Canton (St. Lawrence county) and 8 inches in Malone (Franklin county).||This was the first widespread winter storm of the 2007-08 season, which resulted in numerous vehicle accidents as well as cancellations of schools, businesses and civic organizations.

A low pressure system tracked from the southern Plains, into the central Great Lakes on Sunday, December 23rd, while intensifying. The low pressure system then moved into eastern Canada by Monday, December 24th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system swept east across eastern New York and western New England late Sunday afternoon and evening.||Strong to locally damaging winds occurred ahead of, and with the cold frontal passage on Sunday.

Brisk northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph accompanied the steadier snowfall in the late afternoon and evening hours and that resulted in considerable blowing and drifting of the snow with greatly reduced visibilities.||Storm total snowfall accumulations across northern New York was 9 to 15 inches with some locally higher amounts. Some of the higher snowfall totals included; 17 inches in Ellenburg Depot (Clinton county), 16 inches in Potsdam (St. Lawrence county) and 14 inches in Whippleville (Franklin county).

A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread significant snowfall into eastern New York and western New England, beginning Thursday morning, and persisting into Thursday evening. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches within the Schoharie Valley and the Capital Region, with 6 to 11 inches across the eastern Catskills, and the mid Hudson Valley region.||Further north, snowfall amounts were somewhat lighter, ranging from 3 to 6 inches for areas within the Mohawk Valley and across the western and southern Adirondacks, with 4 to 8 inches near and south of the Mohawk River, including the northern portions of the Capital Region, and the Helderbergs.||The moderate to heavy snowfall created treacherous travel conditions for the Thursday evening commute, leaving some roads impassable, and also resulted in the early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread moderate snowfall into eastern New York, beginning early Thursday afternoon, and persisting into Thursday evening. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches for areas within the Mohawk Valley and across the western and southern Adirondacks, with 4 to 8 inches near and south of the Mohawk River, including the northern portions of the Capital Region, and the Helderbergs.||This moderate snowfall led to treacherous driving conditions for the Thursday evening commute, and led to early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southeast United States to off the Middle Atlantic coast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley before tracking across Southern Pennsylvania to Southern New England. Snow overspread much of the area on the evening of the 15th, before mixing with and changing over to sleet and freezing rain. The highest snowfall totals occurred across the Northern Finger Lakes, Western Mohawk Valley and Tug Hill Plateau, where persistent wrap around snow occurred as the storm redeveloped into a major winter storm on the East Coast. In these areas, snowfall amounts ranged between 10 and 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought 9 to 15 inches of snow to the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to northeast winds blowing across a long distance combined with high astronomical tides caused significant beach erosion and tidal flooding across parts of coastal Southeast NY.  Tidal departures ranged from 2 to 4 feet above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread beach erosion, some significant, along with coastal flooding occurred across parts of Long Island's south fork.  A dock at Maidstone Harbor Marina in East Hampton's Three Mile Harbor was completely submerged an hour after high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Lower Mississippi Valley Wednesday evening, December 12, 2007, then tracked northeast toward the Middle Atlantic States. As the upper trough approached the Eastern Seaboard on Thursday morning, December 13, a secondary low developed along the Middle Atlantic coast. This low tracked to the south and east of Long Island during the evening hours. Polar high pressure preceding the storm dropped southeast across eastern Canada and into the Northeast Wednesday night, providing enough cold air for a predominatly snow event for the interior and a wintry mix at the coast.  Orange and Putnam counties in southeast New York received 4 to 8 inches of snow.|In Orange County, Montgomery and Walden received 8 inches, while in Putnam County, Fahnstock State Park received 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system tracked across upstate New York on Sunday, December 2nd, while a secondary storm developed and gained strength along the New England coast. These two systems brought a mix of precipitation to upstate New York. Many areas of upstate New York changed over to rain, but in Oneida County, the precipitation remained freezing rain, with around one-half inch of ice reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half inch of ice was measured in New Hartford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold northwest flow across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario brought a moderate lake effect snow event to areas east of the lake. Snow totals were heaviest across Oneida County, where amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches. Further south, amounts were less with 5 to 10 inch amounts more common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spotters across the area reported snowfall amounts of 17 inches at Stokes Hill and Westernville, 15 inches at Point Rock and 15.6 inches at Camden from a heavy lake effect snow band that affected the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spotters reported moderate snowfall accumulations, generally north of the New York State Thruway. Amounts included 15.5 inches at Holland Patent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold northwest flow across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario brought a moderate lake effect snow event to areas east of the lake. Snow totals were heaviest across Oneida County, where amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches. Further south, amounts were less with 5 to 10 inch amounts more common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spotters reported snow accumulations ranging from 6 to 8 inches across areas generally north of the city of Syracuse. Snowfall amounts included 8.1 inches at Clay, 8 inches in East Syracuse and 7.9 inches at the Syracuse airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Lake Effect snow band affected portions of southern Herkimer County from Friday afternoon on January 19th, and persisted through the early morning hours of Saturday January 20th.|Isolated snowfall amounts of up to 10 inches occurred within the most persistent and intense portion of this band, with 10 inches reported in Salisbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Lake Effect snow band affected portions of southern Herkimer County from early Friday afternoon on January 19th, and persisted through the early morning hours of Saturday January 20th.|Isolated snowfall amounts of up to 10 inches occurred within the most persistent and intense portion of this band, with 10 inches reported in Salisbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly settled south across the region late Saturday, January 13th, before becoming stationary across the northern Mid Atlantic states. A wave of low pressure then traveled northeast along this front, and produced a mix of freezing rain and sleet across the region Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly settled south across the region late Saturday, January 13th, before becoming stationary across the northern Mid Atlantic states. A wave of low pressure then traveled northeast along this front, and produced a mix of freezing rain and sleet across the region Sunday morning.|Ice accretion from the freezing rain was up to two tenths of an inch. This ice created hazardous travel conditions across the region Sunday morning. |As a result of this hazardous travel, one individual, a 22 year old male, got out of his car after an accident on the eastbound side of the Patroon Island Bridge at 2:15 AM EST. As he waited on the side of the bridge, another vehicle slid into his path, forcing the man to climb onto the bridge railing, when he inadvertently fell about 90 feet to the road below, and was later pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the midwest moved east, reaching south central New York State around midday on Monday, January 15th, and then moved off the southern New England coast by Monday evening. Cold low level air remained in place as precipitation overspread the region early Monday morning, producing a mix of freezing rain and some sleet. Significant icing occurred from the freezing rain during Monday, leading to widespread power outages from downed trees and tree limbs, and from power transformers which shorted out. Although the freezing rain tapered off Monday evening, strengthening winds in the wake of the storm continued to down tree limbs and exacerbate power outages across the region into Monday night.|This ice storm had a significant impact on travel and economy across the region, with areas immediately north of the Mohawk River, including the Saratoga Springs and Lake George regions, and the western Mohawk Valley being hardest hit.|Estimates of 85,000 customers were affected by power outages at the height of the ice storm Monday afternoon and evening in New York State, with the majority of outages in Saratoga county. Some of these customers did not receive power back until Thursday, January 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into east central New York State late Thursday night on January 25th, and remained in place into Friday, January 26th. Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and ten degrees below zero, with some temperatures as low as 15 degrees below zero across higher elevations of the Adirondacks.|In addition, northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph produced wind chills as low as 25 to 30 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into east central New York State late Thursday night on January 25th, and remained in place into Friday, January 26th. Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and fifteen degrees below zero. |The coldest temperature recorded in southern Washington county was 13 degrees below zero in Cambridge.|In addition, northwest winds of 5 to 10 mph produced wind chills as low as 10 to 20 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the midwest moved east, reaching south central New York State around midday on Monday, January 15th, and then moved off the southern New England coast by Monday evening. Cold low level air remained in place as precipitation overspread the region early Monday morning, producing a mix of freezing rain and some sleet. Significant icing occurred from the freezing rain during Monday, leading to widespread power outages from downed trees and tree limbs, and from power transformers which shorted out. Although the freezing rain tapered off Monday evening, strengthening winds in the wake of the storm continued to down tree limbs and exacerbate power outages across the region into Monday night.|This ice storm had a significant impact on travel and economy across the region, with areas immediately north of the Mohawk River, including the Saratoga Springs and Lake George regions, and the western Mohawk Valley being hardest hit.|Estimates of 85,000 customers were affected by power outages at the height of the ice storm Monday afternoon and evening in New York State, with the majority of outages in Saratoga county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure traveled along a stationary boundary draped across northern New York on the 15th. Snow began during the early morning hours of the 15th and mixed with sleet along the Canadian border, but changed to sleet and freezing rain in the Adirondacks before precipitation ended by early evening.|Total snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 6 inches along the Canadian border to 4 inches in the southern Adirondacks, where an additional 1/4 inch of ice accumulation occurred as well. There were numerous vehicle accidents as well as scattered power outages due to the snow and ice. Some specific snowfall amounts included: 6 inches in Mooers and Plattsburgh (Clinton), Duane Center (Franklin) and Madrid (St. Lawrence)...5 inches in Malone (Franklin), Colton, Massena and Potsdam (St. Lawrence).

A powerful and large storm in New Brunswick and the Canadian Maritimes on the 19th and 20th, delivered a cold and unstable air flow into the Adirondacks. This unstable northwest flow accounted for upslope snow conditions to develop on the northwest facing slopes of Clinton and Franklin counties during the evening of the 19th and continued through the morning hours of the 20th. Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches occurred, including 6 inches at Dannermora and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton), 11 inches at Vermontville (Franklin) and 12 inches at Duane (Franklin).

A weak area of low pressure moved across Ontario and Quebec provinces in Canada during the morning and afternoon of the 1st. Mild, moist air traveled over a seasonably cool airmass across New York and this resulted in a period of freezing rain from around Midnight to just before dawn. Freezing rain accumulated up to 1/4 of an inch across northern New York resulting in slick roads and several vehicle accidents.

An arctic cold front moved across northern New York on the 24th and delivered very cold temperatures of 10 to 25 degrees below zero by the morning of the 25th. However, on the night of the 25th into the morning of the 26th, a secondary cold front combined with a strengthening area of low pressure near New Brunswick, accounted for the combination of brisk northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph and temperatures 10 to 20 degrees below zero for wind chill readings of 25 to 35 degrees below zero.|Some morning lows on the 25th included: -26 degrees in Saranac Lake (Franklin), -22 degrees in Canton (St. Lawrence), -19 degrees in Tupper Lake (Franklin), -18 degrees in Malone (Franklin), Ogdensburg (St. Lawrence), -17 degrees in Massena (St. Lawrence) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton) and -13 degrees in Newcomb (Essex).|Some morning lows on the 26th included: -18 degrees in Tupper Lake, -16 degrees in Malone and Canton and -14 degrees in Ellenburg Depot, but northwest winds of 10 to 15 mph created wind chill values of 25 to 35 degrees below zero.|The cold wave diminished slightly on the 27th-29th, due to a slight airmass modification and clouds across the region, but it still remained some 10 to 20 degrees below normal. However, another arctic front pushed across the area on the 29th with a replenishment of arctic air that brought early morning low temperatures on the 30th of 15 to 30 degrees below zero. A strong low-level jet stream (wind energy) delivered an unseasonably warm airmass across northern New York during the morning and afternoon of the 6th, with temperatures in the 50s and 60s. Surface low pressure moved across southern Quebec, while an associated cold front moved across northern New York during the afternoon of the 6th. This cold front briefly captured some of these strong winds and produced localized wind damage in the form of dozens of downed trees and powerlines in and around Keeseville, New York (Clinton county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees and powerlines with approximately 3000 homes without electricity across much of southern Clinton county, concentrated around Keeseville.

A cold northwest flow across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario brought a moderate lake effect snow event to areas east of the lake. Snow totals were heaviest across Oneida County, where amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches. Further south, amounts were less with 5 to 10 inch amounts more common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spotter amounts generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches across areas along the New York State Thruway and north. Snowfall amounts included 8 inches at Morrisville and 6.5 inches at Canastota.

Mild, moist air traveled over a seasonably cool airmass across New York and this resulted in a period of freezing rain from around Midnight to just before dawn. A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread significant snowfall into eastern New York and western New England, beginning Thursday morning, and persisting into Thursday evening. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches within the Schoharie Valley and the Capital Region, with 6 to 11 inches across the eastern Catskills, and the mid Hudson Valley region.||Further north, snowfall amounts were somewhat lighter, ranging from 3 to 6 inches for areas within the Mohawk Valley and across the western and southern Adirondacks, with 4 to 8 inches near and south of the Mohawk River, including the northern portions of the Capital Region, and the Helderbergs.||The moderate to heavy snowfall created treacherous travel conditions for the Thursday evening commute, leaving some roads impassable, and also resulted in the early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary boundary over the Ohio Valley on Sunday, December 9th, and moved east northeast to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning on December 10th.||This system spread a swath of snow and sleet across northern areas, with sleet and freezing rain further south from late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning.||Snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches, in addition to several tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain. ||This wintry mix led to hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary boundary over the Ohio Valley on Sunday, December 9th, and moved east northeast to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning on December 10th. ||Snow and sleet developed across the region Sunday evening, before changing to light freezing rain later Sunday night into Monday morning. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches across the Mohawk Valley region, to an inch or less across the Capital Region and the Lake George and Saratoga Springs region. However, for areas south of the Mohawk River, ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from one tenth of an inch to one third of an inch by the time the precipitation ended Monday morning.||This wintry mix led to hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to either delayed openings, or closings of many schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the western Great Lakes on Sunday, December 2nd, moved rapidly east into central New York State by Monday morning on December 3rd, before moving east northeast and intensifying in the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday December 4th.||As a warm front associated with this storm system approached from the southwest, a swath of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed across much of east central New York Sunday afternoon, and persisted into early Monday morning before tapering off as scattered rain and snow showers.||A significant accumulation of snow and ice occurred across portions of the upper Hudson Valley, the Mohawk Valley and the southern Adirondacks from this storm system. In these areas, several inches of snow and sleet fell, in addition to between one half of an inch, and three quarters of an inch of ice accretion. Further south, snow and sleet accumulations generally were between 1 and 2 inches, in addition to a few tenths of an inch of ice accretion.||This wintry mix led to hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed Sunday evening, before changing to freezing rain late Sunday night, and then ending as rain and snow showers Monday morning.||Snow and sleet amounts of 2 to 5 inches fell, with ice accretion from freezing rain of up to one third of an inch.||This wintry mix led to hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system traveled from the lower Mississippi Valley into the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday, December 15th. This low then redeveloped just off the Delmarva peninsula by Sunday morning, December 16th, and intensified rapidly, becoming the primary low center. This low then tracked into the Canadian maritimes by Monday morning.||This storm system brought a significant mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region, beginning early Sunday morning, and persisting into Sunday night.||Across the southern and western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley region, as well as the Capital Region, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and the Helderbergs, snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches. Further south, snow and sleet amounts of 2 to 6 inches fell across the mid Hudson Valley and southeast Catskills, in addition to several tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain.||The heavy snow and sleet accumulations led to the closings of numerous schools and businesses Monday morning, and also created treacherous driving conditions from Sunday into Monday morning.||In addition, across the central Taconics, the combination of several tenths of an inch of ice accretion and gusty winds led to downed trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the early morning hours of Sunday, December 16th, and ended early Monday morning. Sleet mixed in with the snow at times, particularly during Sunday afternoon and evening.||Total snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 9 inches, with 9 inches reported at Jefferson, and 5.5 inches reported in Schoharie. ||The heavy snow and sleet led to numerous school and business closings Monday morning. In addition, the snow and sleet created hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level and surface storm system moved from the Great Lakes on December 23rd across Ontario and Quebec during the 24th.  Accompanying this system were strong winds in excess of 70 to 80 mph between 3000 and 6000 feet above the surface. Meanwhile, at the surface, a strong warm and cold front associated with this powerful storm moved across northern New York during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. The development of rain showers along the cold front during the evening hours allowed for some of these strong winds to reach the surface which caused localized wind damage in the form of downed branches, trees and scattered power outages as well as damaged loose holiday lawn ornaments in southern portions of the northern Adirondacks in communities like Fine (St. Lawrence county), Tupper Lake and Saranac Lake (Franklin county) and Lake Placid (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches and power lines reported downed by high winds between Tupper Lake and Saranac Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the passage of a strong cold front in combination with a melting snow pack caused the Mahwah River at Suffern to overflow its' banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mahwah River at Suffern exceeded its' flood stage of 4 feet on Dec. 23, 2007 at 8:45 pm with a stage of 4.12 feet. The river crested at 5.65 feet on Dec. 24, 2007 at 12:30 am and returned within its banks on Dec 24, 2007 at 1:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to northeast winds blowing across a long distance combined with high astronomical tides caused significant beach erosion and tidal flooding across parts of coastal Southeast NY.  Tidal departures ranged from 2 to 4 feet above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported significant over washes in Asharoken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system traveled from the lower Mississippi Valley into the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday, December 15th. This low then redeveloped just off the Delmarva peninsula by Sunday morning, December 16th, and intensified rapidly, becoming the primary low center. Further south, snow and sleet amounts of 2 to 6 inches fell across the mid Hudson Valley and southeast Catskills, in addition to several tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain.||The heavy snow and sleet accumulations led to the closings of numerous schools and businesses Monday morning, and also created treacherous driving conditions from Sunday into Monday morning.||In addition, across the central Taconics, the combination of several tenths of an inch of ice accretion and gusty winds led to downed trees and powerlines.

Sleet mixed in with the snow at times, particularly during Sunday afternoon and evening.||Total snowfall amounts generally ranged from 8 to 11 inches, with 10.3 inches reported at Saratoga Springs and at Gansevoort.||The heavy snow and sleet led to numerous school and business closings Monday morning. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Lower Mississippi Valley Wednesday evening, December 12, 2007, then tracked northeast toward the Middle Atlantic States. As the upper trough approached the Eastern Seaboard on Thursday morning, December 13, a secondary low developed along the Middle Atlantic coast. This low tracked to the south and east of Long Island during the evening hours. Polar high pressure preceding the storm dropped southeast across eastern Canada and into the Northeast Wednesday night, providing enough cold air for a predominatly snow event for the interior and a wintry mix at the coast.  Orange and Putnam counties in southeast New York received 4 to 8 inches of snow.|In Orange County, Montgomery and Walden received 8 inches, while in Putnam County, Fahnstock State Park received 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and weak area of low pressure moved east from the Great Lakes and then across northern New York during the night of December 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. Light snow overspread northern New York by mid afternoon on the 19th and exited the region by midday on the 20th. Snowfall accumulations from this system was generally 2 to 4 inches across northern New York.

A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary boundary over the Ohio Valley on Sunday, December 9th, and moved east northeast to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning on December 10th.||This system spread a swath of snow and sleet across northern areas, with sleet and freezing rain further south from late Sunday afternoon into Monday morning.||Snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches, in addition to several tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain. ||This wintry mix led to hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary boundary over the Ohio Valley on Sunday, December 9th, and moved east northeast to a position just south of Long Island by Monday morning on December 10th. ||Snow and sleet developed across the region Sunday evening, before changing to light freezing rain later Sunday night into Monday morning. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches across the Mohawk Valley region, to an inch or less across the Capital Region and the Lake George and Saratoga Springs region. However, for areas south of the Mohawk River, ice accretion from freezing rain ranged from one tenth of an inch to one third of an inch by the time the precipitation ended Monday morning.||This wintry mix led to hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to either delayed openings, or closings of many schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the western Great Lakes on Sunday, December 2nd, moved rapidly east into central New York State by Monday morning on December 3rd, before moving east northeast and intensifying in the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday December 4th.||As a warm front associated with this storm system approached from the southwest, a swath of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed across much of east central New York Sunday afternoon, and persisted into early Monday morning before tapering off as scattered rain and snow showers.||A significant accumulation of snow and ice occurred across portions of the upper Hudson Valley, the Mohawk Valley and the southern Adirondacks from this storm system. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level and surface storm system moved from the Great Lakes on December 23rd across Ontario and Quebec during the 24th.  Accompanying this system were strong winds in excess of 70 to 80 mph between 3000 and 6000 feet above the surface. Meanwhile, at the surface, a strong warm and cold front associated with this powerful storm moved across northern New York during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 23rd. The development of rain showers along the cold front during the evening hours allowed for some of these strong winds to reach the surface which caused localized wind damage in the form of downed branches, trees and scattered power outages as well as damaged loose holiday lawn ornaments in southern portions of the northern Adirondacks in communities like Fine (St. Lawrence county), Tupper Lake and Saranac Lake (Franklin county) and Lake Placid (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches and power lines reported downed by high winds in southeast St. Lawrence county near Fine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread significant snowfall into eastern New York and western New England, beginning Thursday morning, and persisting into Thursday evening. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches within the Schoharie Valley and the Capital Region, with 6 to 11 inches across the eastern Catskills, and the mid Hudson Valley region.||Further north, snowfall amounts were somewhat lighter, ranging from 3 to 6 inches for areas within the Mohawk Valley and across the western and southern Adirondacks, with 4 to 8 inches near and south of the Mohawk River, including the northern portions of the Capital Region, and the Helderbergs.||The moderate to heavy snowfall created treacherous travel conditions for the Thursday evening commute, leaving some roads impassable, and also resulted in the early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread moderate snowfall into eastern New York, beginning early Thursday afternoon, and persisting into Thursday evening. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches for areas within the Mohawk Valley and across the western and southern Adirondacks, with 4 to 8 inches near and south of the Mohawk River, including the northern portions of the Capital Region, and the Helderbergs.||This moderate snowfall led to treacherous driving conditions for the Thursday evening commute, and led to early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system traveled from the lower Mississippi Valley into the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday, December 15th. This low then redeveloped just off the Delmarva peninsula by Sunday morning, December 16th, and intensified rapidly, becoming the primary low center. This low then tracked into the Canadian maritimes by Monday morning.||This storm system brought a significant mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region, beginning early Sunday morning, and persisting into Sunday night.||Across the southern and western Adirondacks and western Mohawk Valley region, as well as the Capital Region, the Lake George and Saratoga region, and the Helderbergs, snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches. Further south, snow and sleet amounts of 2 to 6 inches fell across the mid Hudson Valley and southeast Catskills, in addition to several tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain.||The heavy snow and sleet accumulations led to the closings of numerous schools and businesses Monday morning, and also created treacherous driving conditions from Sunday into Monday morning.||In addition, across the central Taconics, the combination of several tenths of an inch of ice accretion and gusty winds led to downed trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet developed during the early morning hours of Sunday December 16th, before changing to freezing rain by early Sunday afternoon, and then plain rain by late Sunday afternoon. The rain then changed back to snow late Sunday night, before ending early Monday morning.||Total snow and sleet amounts of 2 to 4 inches fell during this storm, in addition to a few tenths of an inch of ice accretion from freezing rain.||The wintry mix created treacherous travel conditions Sunday into Monday morning, and also led to numerous school and business closings Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first true arctic air mass of the season swept into western and central New York on the heels of a major synoptic storm during the first few days of December. A strong west to northwest flow followed the storm early on the third and changed to rain to snow from west to east during the morning hours. The snow was heavy at times across higher elevations south of Buffalo during the afternoon and evening where several inches fell. Very strong northwest winds brought intense streamers in from Lake Huron across Lake Erie. Off Lake Ontario, multiple band streamers developed by late Monday (the third) which continued through most of Tuesday.  The activity wound down late Tuesday, the fourth. Off Reports downwind of Lake Ontario included: Constantia 21���; Hooker 18���; West Monroe 16���; Rochester, Newark and Waterloo 14���; Avon and Stanley 7���.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Tuesday February 13th, and then tracked northeast, reaching the mid Atlantic coast by Wednesday morning on February 14th, while intensifying. The storm continued to strengthen during the day on February 14th, before moving off the New England coast by Wednesday night.|The storm spread a swath of very heavy snowfall across eastern New York and western New England, which started during the late evening hours of Tuesday, and tapered off late Wednesday night.|Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 feet across the hardest hit areas of New York State, which included the Schoharie Valley, western Mohawk Valley, and southern Adirondacks.  Amounts as high as 40 to 42 inches occurred in portions of western Montgomery County and southern Herkimer County. Slightly lower amounts occurred across the lower mid Hudson Valley, where sleet mixed in with the snow.|This storm significantly impacted travel and commerce across the region from Wednesday through Thursday, as many schools and businesses were closed.  Many roadways became impassable by Wednesday afternoon.|In addition, in Wappingers Falls in western Dutchess County, a roof collapse occurred due to the weight of heavy sleet and snow accumulation on a structure.  Although 20 people were displaced, there were no reports of injuries.|Strong winds also created near blizzard conditions at times during Wednesday afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southeast states during Thursday afternoon on March 15th, and then tracked northeast while intensifying, reaching the Delmarva coast by Friday evening on March 16th, and off the New England coast by Saturday morning on March 17th.|This storm brought widespread snowfall amounts of 10 to 20 inches across eastern New York and western New England, with some amounts of near two feet across the eastern Catskill region, as well as within a band extending across southern Columbia, and northern Dutchess counties of New York.  The snow began across the lower mid Hudson Valley during the mid morning hours of Friday, March 16th, and spread into the Capital Region during midday.  The snow did not reach portions of the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks until the late afternoon hours, as dry air remained in place longer, allowing the precipitation to remain aloft for several hours before reaching the ground.  This also allowed snowfall amounts to be less across this region, with generally 6 to 9 inches occurring.|Further to the southeast, some sleet mixed in with the snow, beginning during the early evening hours of Friday, and persisting into Friday night, keeping snowfall amounts a bit less across the lower mid Hudson Valley, where an average of 8 to 12 inches occurred.|This storm had significant adverse impacts to travel and commerce across the region from Friday afternoon into early Saturday morning. Travel was treacherous for the Friday evening commute, with numerous accidents reported.
