A persistent southwest flow of arctic air traveled across the relatively milder Lake Ontario during the late afternoon of the 3rd and continuing into the morning hours of the 4th. This led to the development of lake effect snow bands that remained persistent until shifting south of the area during midday of the 4th. Snowfall rates greater than 3 inches an hour and brisk winds of 10 to 20 mph with higher gusts created blizzard-like conditions at times. The greatest snowfall occurred in the St. Lawrence county portion of the Adirondacks with reports of 1 to 2 feet, while on the periphery of this axis...6 to 12 inches of snow fell in the Adirondacks of southern Franklin county and the foothills of southern St. Lawrence county.|Some specific snowfall totals included: 24 inches in Star Lake (St. Lawrence), 20 inches in Pitcairn (St. Lawrence), 18 inches in Fine (St. Lawrence), 16 inches in Oswegatchie (St. Lawrence) and 8 inches in Saranac Lake and Tupper Lake (Franklin).

Low pressure developed across the lower Mississippi river valley on the morning of the 13th and moved into the central Appalachians by the early morning hours of the 14th. During the early morning hours of the 14th, a coastal low quickly developed near the Delmarva peninsula and rapidly intensified, as it moved northeast across southeast Massachusetts and into the Gulf of Maine by the evening of the 14th.|Snow overspread the region during the early morning hours of the 14th and fell heavy at times, with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour during the mid afternoon into the early evening hours before dissipating before midnight. In addition to the heavy snowfall, brisk winds of 15 to 25 mph caused near whiteout conditions at times along with considerable blowing and drifting of the snow, making roads nearly impassable and blocking heating system vents. Further, temperatures in the single numbers above zero combined with these brisk winds created wind chill values of 10 degrees below zero or colder. States of Emergencies were declared in Essex and Clinton counties. There were several indirect related injuries due to vehicle accidents, cardiac arrest from snow removal, as well as carbon monoxide poisoning due to blocked ventilations.|Snowfall totals ranged from 15 inches in the Adirondacks of St. Lawrence and Franklin counties to nearly 40 inches in the Adirondacks of southern Essex county with 20 to 30 inches across much of the Champlain valley. Some specific snowfall totals included; 15 inches in Edwards (St. Lawrence), 16 inches in Ellenburg Depot (Clinton), Paul Smiths and Saranac Lake (Franklin), 20 inches in Plattsburgh (Clinton), 21 inches in Tupper Lake (Franklin), 23 inches in AuSable Forks (Essex), 26 inches in Newcomb (Essex), 27 inches in Mineville (Essex) and Peru (Clinton), 30 inches in Moriah (Essex) with 38 inches in Minerva (Essex).

This lake effect event started dramatically on the weekend of February 3-4 in association with a surge of arctic air and then continue all week off Lake Ontario as ripples in the low level circulation shifted winds and kept bands of snow moving up and down off Lake Ontario. Lake Erie was in the process of freezing up all week, so although conditions were favorable only an inch or two fell each day after the initial burst on the opening weekend.|The activity began on Saturday morning (the third) on a southwest flow as winds backed ahead of an arctic front. Several inches fell across metro Buffalo as well as Watertown. The front whipped through later in the day with another burst of snow, then intense lake effect bands set up overnight and lingered all day Sunday across Buffalo���s south Towns as well as southern Jefferson and Oswego counties. An arctic air mass caused subfreezing temperatures across the local area for 5 consecutive days; from February 4th through February 8th.|During this time, the daily mean temperature averaged nearly 15 degrees below normal.|Based on a report by the NYC Medical Examiner's Office, hypothermia, was a direct cause of at least 11 fatalities during and after this period of extreme cold in New York City.

A low pressure system developed over the southern plains on February 12th, and intensified rapidly as it neared the East Coast on the night of the 13th. The storm then continued to strengthen as it moved up the Atlantic Seaboard during the day on February 14th. The storm spread snow into central New York beginning the evening of Tuesday, the 13th. The snow continued heavy at times through the 14th and gradually tapered off to snow showers on the 15th as the storm pulled northeast past the Gulf of Maine. Some sleet mixed with the snow for brief periods of time. The snow became heavy with near blizzard conditions at times over the Finger Lakes and central southern tier of New York during the early morning hours of the 14th. The heavy snow and near blizzard conditions shifted east to the upper Susquehanna Region of New York and western Mohawk Valley by the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind the storm late on the 14th and through the 15th which led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. This hampered snow plowing and snow cleanup operations. As a result, many roads and highways were closed during the height of the snowstorm. Many counties and municipalities declared snow emergencies. Storm total snowfall amounts across much of central New York ranged between 15 and 30 inches. Less snow fell in Sullivan County, New York where more sleet was reported. This kept snowfall amounts down between 8 and 12 inches in this area. The heaviest snowfall from this storm occurred in Delaware and Otsego counties where between 2 and 3 feet was common. The highest snowfall was reported in Roseboom where 39 inches of snow occurred and Springfield where 38 inches fell. The weight of the snow caused several roofs to collapse.

Winds turned northwesterly behind the departing Valentine's Day snowstorm on the 15th and 16th. These winds brought arctic air southward over the warmer waters of Lake Ontario. This set-up led to another major lake effect snowstorm for Oneida, Madison and Onondaga counties on the 15th and 16th. This heavy lake effect snow fell on top of the 15 to 30 inches of snow that occurred on the 14th. Lake effect snow totals between the 15th and 16th ranged from 1 to 2 feet across far northern Onondaga, and far northern Madison counties. 8 to 14 inches of snow fell across the northern third of Oneida county with 6 to 12 inches in central Oneida county and lesser amounts in the southern part of this county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow brought 1 to 2 feet of snow to the far northern part of the county with generally 5 to 10 inches of snow across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy lake effect snow shifted south into the northern half of Oneida county around 10 pm during the evening of the 4th and continued through the daylight hours of the 5th, ending during the morning of the 6th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour occurred at times, leading to near blizzard conditions. Total snowfall accumulations for this lake effect snow episode ranged from two to three feet over the northern third of Oneida county, north of the New York State Thruway. The highest snowfall accumulations were in an area bounded by Remsen, Lake Delta, Camden, Westdale, Florence and Point Rock. Over the central third of the county close to the thruway between 9 and 16 inches of snow fell. Much less snow fell well south of the Thruway, with virtually no snow occurring in the far southern end of Oneida county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall ranged from 10 to 18 inches across the far northern part of Oneida county between 7 am EST February 4th to 7 am EST February 5th. Another 10 to 20 inches of lake effect snow fell between 7 am EST February 5th and 7 am EST February 6th with additional snows after 7 am EST on the 6th bringing storm totals to between 2 to 3 feet across the northern third of Oneida county. The heaviest lake effect snow was reported in the northwest part of the county near Westdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major lake effect snowstorm struck north central New York from the 8th to the 10th. Particularly hard hit was the northern third of Oneida County which saw 2 to 4 feet of snowfall. This snow fell on top of 2 to 3 feet which fell between the 4th and the 6th. These two lake effect snowstorms brought lake effect snowfall totals to between 4 and 6 feet in many areas of far northern Oneida county. The highest snowfall between February 4th and 10th occurred in the northwest part of Oneida County where 75 inches of snow was reported at Westdale. The central portion of Oneida County along the thruway corridor was spared the heaviest snowfall from this second lake effect snowstorm. This second major lake effect snowstorm also affected far northern Onondaga County and far northern Madison county where between 10 and 20 inches of snow fell between the 8th and the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another major lake effect snowstorm struck northern Oneida county with between 2 and 4 feet of additional snowfall from the 8th to the 10th. This snow fell on top of 2 to 3 feet of lake effect that fell between the 4th and the 6th, Some parts of northwest Oneida county saw over 6 feet of snow from these two lake effect episodes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front swept across northern New York during the afternoon of the 5th and delivered frigid temperatures along with blustery winds. Temperatures plummeted to below zero late on the 5th  and were 10 to 20 degrees below zero by dawn on the 6th. These frigid temperatures, accompanied by winds of 15 to 30 mph created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 degrees below zero. Brisk winds with temperatures around zero continued through the daylight hours of the 6th with wind chill readings in the 20s to around 30 degrees below zero. The winds subsided after sunset on the 6th but it remained extremely cold through the morning of the 7th with overnight minimum temperatures of 15 to 35 degrees below zero.|Some morning lows on the 6th included: -17 degrees at both Saranac Lake and Tupper Lake (Franklin), -16 degrees at Newcomb (Essex) and Malone (Franklin) with -14 degrees at Canton (St. Lawrence) and -12 degrees at Massena (St. Lawrence) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton).|Some morning lows on the 7th included: -35 degrees in Saranac Lake (Franklin), -31 degrees at Newcomb (Essex), -26 degrees in Ogdensburg (St. Lawrence), -22 degrees in Chazy (Clinton), -21 degrees in Plattsburgh (Clinton), -20 degrees in Canton (St. Lawrence) and -19 degrees in Massena (St. Lawrence) and Malone (Franklin).

As a ridge of high pressure extending from north of the Great Lakes southeast across southern New England moved slowly northeast, a low pressure system developed off the Carolina coast.  As this low moved north to northeast just off the mid Atlantic coast, it rapidly intensified during Wednesday morning.  This low moved across eastern Long Island during the early afternoon hours. |These systems produced snow, sleet, freezing rain, rain, strong gusty winds, and minor tidal flooding across the region. |Light snow developed from southwest to northeast during Tuesday evening between 5 p.m. and 10 p.m.  It mixed with sleet and freezing rain, first across eastern Long Island between midnight and 3 a.m. and then across the remainder of the area between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m.  By 6 a.m., a mixed bag of sleet and freezing rain occurred everywhere with temperatures ranging from the lower 30s across eastern Long Island, to 10 degrees in the Lower Hudson Valley, to 19 degrees at Central Park. |As the deepening low moved more to the northeast, cold air was locked in west of the storm track, prompting the issuance of Ice Storm Warnings for Brooklyn, Queens, Nassau, and NW Suffolk between 10 a.m. and 10:15 a.m.  Six hour precipitation totals between 7 a.m. and 1 p.m. were between 0.50 and 0.75 inches.  This fell as mainly plain rain across the twin forks, and much of Suffolk County; a combo of sleet and freezing rain fell across Nassau; and mainly sleet with some freezing rain elsewhere including NYC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began during the late evening on Tuesday Feb. 13, then mixed with sleet during the early morning hours. A moderate to heavy sleet developed Wednesday morning, Feb. 14, and continued into the early afternoon before ending as some light snow. The accumulation of 2 to 4 inches of sleet caused major mass transit problems along with severe delays at the major airports.

Some sleet mixed with the snow for brief periods of time. The snow became heavy with near blizzard conditions at times over the Finger Lakes and central southern tier of New York during the early morning hours of the 14th. The heavy snow and near blizzard conditions shifted east to the upper Susquehanna Region of New York and western Mohawk Valley by the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind the storm late on the 14th and through the 15th which led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. This hampered snow plowing and snow cleanup operations. As a result, many roads and highways were closed during the height of the snowstorm. Many counties and municipalities declared snow emergencies. Storm total snowfall amounts across much of central New York ranged between 15 and 30 inches. Less snow fell in Sullivan County, New York where more sleet was reported. This kept snowfall amounts down between 8 and 12 inches in this area. The heaviest snowfall from this storm occurred in Delaware and Otsego counties where between 2 and 3 feet was common. The highest snowfall was reported in Roseboom where 39 inches of snow occurred and Springfield where 38 inches fell. The weight of the snow caused several roofs to collapse.

A band of heavy lake effect snow shifted south into the northern half of Oneida county around 10 pm during the evening of the 4th and continued through the daylight hours of the 5th, ending during the morning of the 6th. Snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour occurred at times, leading to near blizzard conditions. Total snowfall accumulations for this lake effect snow episode ranged from two to three feet over the northern third of Oneida county, north of the New York State Thruway. The highest snowfall accumulations were in an area bounded by Remsen, Lake Delta, Camden, Westdale, Florence and Point Rock. Over the central third of the county close to the thruway between 9 and 16 inches of snow fell. Much less snow fell well south of the Thruway, with virtually no snow occurring in the far southern end of Oneida county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snowfall ranged between 9 and 16 inches between 7 am EST February 5th and 7 am EST February 6th across the central third of Oneida county close to the New York State Thruway. The heaviest lake effect snowfall occurred over the northern third of the county with virtually no snow falling in the extreme south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front swept across northern New York during the afternoon of the 5th and delivered frigid temperatures along with blustery winds. These frigid temperatures, accompanied by winds of 15 to 30 mph created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 degrees below zero. Brisk winds with temperatures around zero continued through the daylight hours of the 6th with wind chill readings in the 20s to around 30 degrees below zero. The accumulation of 2 to 4 inches of snow and ice caused major mass transit problems, especially during the morning and evening commutes.

Winds turned northwesterly behind the departing Valentine's Day snowstorm on the 15th and 16th. These winds brought arctic air southward over the warmer waters of Lake Ontario. This set-up led to another major lake effect snowstorm for Oneida, Madison and Onondaga counties on the 15th and 16th. This heavy lake effect snow fell on top of the 15 to 30 inches of snow that occurred on the 14th. Lake effect snow totals between the 15th and 16th ranged from 1 to 2 feet across far northern Onondaga, and far northern Madison counties. 8 to 14 inches of snow fell across the northern third of Oneida county with 6 to 12 inches in central Oneida county and lesser amounts in the southern part of this county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow brought generally 6 to 12 inches of snow to Southern Oneida county. Snow spotters in Utica measured 10 inches.

A low pressure system over the Great Lakes intensified as it moved southeast across central New York and northeast Pennsylvania on the 22nd. Initially, the air was mild ahead of the storm. But a strong cold front pushed through the region in the afternoon, accompanied by snow and wind. As the storm moved off the southern New England coast, snow heavy at times, affected most of eastern New York, including Delaware county. The storm brought between 4 and 11 inches of snow to Delaware county. Roxbury measured 11.1 inches and Stamford received 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavier snow brought between 4 and 11 inches of snow to the county. Roxbury measured 11.1 inches and Stamford received 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major lake effect snowstorm struck north central New York from the 8th to the 10th. Particularly hard hit was the northern third of Oneida County which saw 2 to 4 feet of snowfall. This snow fell on top of 2 to 3 feet which fell between the 4th and the 6th. These two lake effect snowstorms brought lake effect snowfall totals to between 4 and 6 feet in many areas of far northern Oneida county. The highest snowfall between February 4th and 10th occurred in the northwest part of Oneida County where 75 inches of snow was reported at Westdale. The central portion of Oneida County along the thruway corridor was spared the heaviest snowfall from this second lake effect snowstorm. This second major lake effect snowstorm also affected far northern Onondaga County and far northern Madison county where between 10 and 20 inches of snow fell between the 8th and the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snows brought between 10 to 20 inches of snowfall to far northern portions of Onondaga county between the 8th and the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the southern plains on February 12th, and intensified rapidly as it neared the East Coast on the night of the 13th. The storm then continued to strengthen as it moved up the Atlantic Seaboard during the day on February 14th. The storm spread snow into central New York beginning the evening of Tuesday, the 13th. The snow continued heavy at times through the 14th and gradually tapered off to snow showers on the 15th as the storm pulled northeast past the Gulf of Maine. Some sleet mixed with the snow for brief periods of time. The snow became heavy with near blizzard conditions at times over the Finger Lakes and central southern tier of New York during the early morning hours of the 14th. The heavy snow and near blizzard conditions shifted east to the upper Susquehanna Region of New York and western Mohawk Valley by the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Gusty winds to 40 mph developed behind the storm late on the 14th and through the 15th which led to considerable blowing and drifting snow. This hampered snow plowing and snow cleanup operations. As a result, many roads and highways were closed during the height of the snowstorm. Many counties and municipalities declared snow emergencies. Storm total snowfall amounts across much of central New York ranged between 15 and 30 inches. Less snow fell in Sullivan County, New York where more sleet was reported. This kept snowfall amounts down between 8 and 12 inches in this area. The heaviest snowfall from this storm occurred in Delaware and Otsego counties where between 2 and 3 feet was common. The highest snowfall was reported in Roseboom where 39 inches of snow occurred and Springfield where 38 inches fell. The weight of the snow caused several roofs to collapse.

Winds turned northwesterly behind the departing Valentine's Day snowstorm on the 15th and 16th. These winds brought arctic air southward over the warmer waters of Lake Ontario. This set-up led to another major lake effect snowstorm for Oneida, Madison and Onondaga counties on the 15th and 16th. This heavy lake effect snow fell on top of the 15 to 30 inches of snow that occurred on the 14th. Lake effect snow totals between the 15th and 16th ranged from 1 to 2 feet across far northern Onondaga, and far northern Madison counties. 8 to 14 inches of snow fell across the northern third of Oneida county with 6 to 12 inches in central Oneida county and lesser amounts in the southern part of this county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow brought generally 12 to 20 inches of snow to far northern Onondaga county with about 5 to 10 inches of snow across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast into the Ohio Valley and spread a mix of snow and sleet into upstate New York after midnight on the 15th. The snow and sleet changed to freezing rain across most of the area during the morning. The precipitation changed over to all rain across the Finger Lakes and south central New York around midday. However, freezing rain continued to fall across the Mohawk Valley and Southern Tug Hill Plateau through the early afternoon hours. Total ice accumulations ranged from one-half to three-quarters of an inch in many locations. The weight of the ice brought down trees and power lines in scattered localities. Roads also became very slippery and travel was impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow reignited lake effect snows off both Lakes Erie and Ontario Wednesday morning (31st). The activity was very intense off Lake Ontario, focusing near the Oswego-Jefferson County line and extending right across the Tug Hill. Snow fell at 2 to 4 inches per hour and piled up to 1 to 3 feet on the southwestern portion of the plateau. The activity broke up a bit and lifted north in the evening as shear increased. Off Lake Erie, a broader area of disorganized lake snow prevailed through most of the morning before it consolidated into an intense west to east band over Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties at midday. This band lifted north across Buffalo's south Towns by late afternoon, but dissipated right as it reached Buffalo due to increasing southerly shear. The movement of the band precluded heavy accumulations with most areas only seeing heavy snow for an hour or two, with totals generally 4 to 6 inches with a few outliers of up to 8 on the Chautauqua ridge and over to Ellicottville. |Off Lake Ontario, specific snowfall reports included: 35 inches at Redfield, 16 inches at Mannsville, 15 inches as North Osceola, 13 inches as Hooker, and 12 inches at Highmarket.

Cold air moving across the warmer waters of Lake Ontario brought a minor lake effect snow event across areas east of Lake Ontario during the afternoon and evening. Lighter snow showers during the afternoon increased in intensity during the afternoon. The heavier snow fell north of the New York State Thruway across northern Onondaga county. Total snow accumulations ranged around 7 inches in the far northern part of Onondaga county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of lake effect snow brought 6.5 inches of snow to the Town of Clay in northern Onondaga county.

On Cattaraugs Creek, over one hundred residents had to be evacuated at Sunset Bay and Swamp Road in East Otto was closed due to flood waters. The Allegheny River crested nearly four feet above flood stage at Olean and twenty-five residents were rescued from the Portville Squart Apartments. Low pressure off the Delmarva Peninsula moved northeast at deepened off Cape Cod. The large low pressure area brought a blanket of heavy snow across the entire region. The snow began during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 16th and continued through the late night hours of Saturday night. A general eight to ten inches of snow fell across the area with slightly higher amounts downwind of Lakes Erie and Ontario. The heavy snow, which fell on the heels of significant flooding across the western sections, brought a return to mid-winter conditions and was blamed for a rash of automobile accidents.

A cold front approached east central New York during the late evening hours of Monday, April 23rd.  As the cold front interacted with very warm and unstable air over central New York, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed.  These thunderstorms contained strong wind gusts, aided by the presence of relatively dry air in the low levels of the atmosphere, which allowed winds to accelerate downward as falling rain cooled the air rapidly aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down in Frankfort as a result of strong winds from a thunderstorm.

A widespread rainfall occurred from March 15th to 16th as a slow-moving cold front dropped south from Canada and crossed upstate New York from the evening of the 14th to the morning of the 15th. The rain fell on a melting snowpack which caused many rivers, and creeks to exceed flood stage.  Rainfall of 0.75 to 1.50 inches was observed in the Susquehanna and Chemung River basins and the Finger lakes of New York. Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches was observed in the Delaware River Basin of New York. Rivers generally crested 0.5 to 2.0 feet above flood stage. This amounted to minor flooding at most locations.  |The Susquehanna River at Conklin, New York was the only location to report significant flood damage. The river at Conklin crested just above the moderate flood stage of 13.0 feet the evening of the 15th. 20 homes (6 occupied), 1 business, and 5 roads were flooded. An ice jam was associated with this flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Conklin from the morning hours of March 16th to the evening of the 17th. The river at Conklin crested at 13.2 feet which is 2.2 feet over the flood stage of 11 feet. Over an inch of rain combining with snowmelt caused minor flooding initially. An ice jam formed and caused the river to reach its moderate stage of 13 feet, flooding 20 homes (6 occupied), 1 business, and 5 roads. Approximately 1/4 million dollars worth of damage was caused by this flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wrap-around moisture associated with the departing low pressure system that brought snow to upstate NY on March 16th and early on the 17th, combined with moisture from Lake Ontario and the Finger Lakes to produce bands of heavy snow for portions of the Finger Lakes Region. Between 10 and 12 inches of snow fell in the Ithaca area with much of the rest of the southern Finger Lakes Region seeing between 5 and 9 inches of snow.

Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches occurred on the night of the 24th as low pressure tracked across central NY. In addition, mild temperatures remained over the region through the end of the month. The mild temperatures continued melting of snowpack. This amounted to a period of prolonged minor flooding at a few river forecast points from the 25th to the 30th. For much of this time, river levels hovered close to or slightly above flood stage along the main branch of the Susquehanna from Waverly east through the Binghamton area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Conklin from the afternoon of March 25th to the afternoon of the 30th. The river at Conklin crested at 12.6 feet which was 1.6 feet over the flood stage of 11 feet. Continued melting of the snowpack and a period of rainfall on the 24th and 25th led to the minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air advected into the region on Tuesday March 13th into Wednesday 14th. This led to significant snowmelt across much of east central New York, particularly across the western Mohawk valley and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice began to melt and move along many streams, creeks and rivers during this time.|A slow moving cold front then dropped southeast across the region late Wednesday into Thursday, accompanied by moderate to locally heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, with higher amounts of up to, or slightly in excess of one inch across the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks.|The combination of rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding of small streams and creeks across portions of the east central New York State, starting on March 14th, and lingering until March 18th. In addition, localized river flooding also occurred, especially where ice jams developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowmelt combined with moderate rainfall to create flooding in portions of Schoharie county early Thursday morning on March 15th.|The Warnerville cutoff west of Cobleskill was closed off due to flooding of West Creek and Cobleskill Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Waverly/Sayre from the morning of March 27th to the evening of the 29th. The river at Waverly/Sayre crested at 11.1 feet which was .1 feet over the flood stage of 11 feet. Continued melting of the snowpack and a period of rainfall on the 24th and 25th led to the minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northwest into the western Great Lakes from the 1st to the 2nd. This low dragged an associated warm front to southern Pennsylvania by the morning of the 2nd. A second area of low pressure developed along the front over Pennsylvania by early on the 2nd, bringing a mixture of precipitation to upstate New York. The mixed precipitation started out as light drizzle and snow, before transitioning to steady snow and sleet during the evening on the 1st. During the early morning hours of the 2nd, the precipitation changed over to freezing rain and continued for several hours before tapering off during the early afternoon of March 2nd. In central NY, up to 1/2 of an inch of ice fell from the southern Tug Hill Plateau, western Mohawk Valley southeast to the western Catskills. The ice caused sporadic power outages and numerous road closures.
Thunderstorms developed along a warm front and lake breeze convergence zone east of Lake Ontario, bringing a few strong to severe storms across north central New York late in the afternoon.

A widespread rainfall occurred from March 15th to 16th as a slow-moving cold front dropped south from Canada and crossed upstate New York from the evening of the 14th to the morning of the 15th. The rain fell on a melting snowpack which caused many rivers, and creeks to exceed flood stage.  Rainfall of 0.75 to 1.50 inches was observed in the Susquehanna and Chemung River basins and the Finger lakes of New York. Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches was observed in the Delaware River Basin of New York. Rivers generally crested 0.5 to 2.0 feet above flood stage. This amounted to minor flooding at most locations.  |The Susquehanna River at Conklin, New York was the only location to report significant flood damage. The river at Conklin crested just above the moderate flood stage of 13.0 feet the evening of the 15th. 20 homes (6 occupied), 1 business, and 5 roads were flooded. An ice jam was associated with this flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Susquehanna River at Bainbridge from the morning of March 15th to the morning of the 17th. The river at Bainbridge crested at 15.1 feet which was 2.1 feet over the flood stage of 13 feet. Over an inch of rain combining with snowmelt caused the minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wrap-around moisture associated with the departing low pressure system that brought snow to upstate NY on March 16th and early on the 17th, combined with moisture from Lake Ontario and the Finger Lakes to produce bands of heavy snow for portions of the Finger Lakes Region. Between 10 and 12 inches of snow fell in the Ithaca area with much of the rest of the southern Finger Lakes Region seeing between 5 and 9 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure gathered strength and moisture while tracking northeast over the Carolinas and eastern Virginia during the morning and afternoon of the 16th. This low reached the Delmarva peninsula by the evening of the 16th and intensified further becoming a major late season winter storm. This storm spread snow, heavy at times, to upstate New York during the morning and early afternoon of the 16th. The snow ended by the early morning hours of the 17th as the storm tracked out to sea well away from the region. Total snowfall accumulations range from 10 to 15 inches over the Catskills of central NY to between 4 and 8 inches across the rest of central NY.

A cold front slipped south of the tri-state area during the day on Thursday, March 15.  This allowed colder air to work into the region after several days of anomalously warm temperatures.  Farther to the south, along the Gulf Coast, low pressure developed and deepened as it tracked northeast along the boundary.  The deepening low tracked to near the Mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, March 16, to just east of Montauk Point Saturday morning, March 17. |Initially, the precipitation started off as a light mix of snow, sleet, and rain during the early morning hours, but, as the colder air continued to deepen, the preciptation fell primarily as a mix of snow and sleet during the daytime hours, falling moderate to occasionally heavy at times. The precipitation fell primariliy as snow across the Lower Hudson Valley, while New York City and Long Island were primarily a mix of sleet and snow.  During the evening hours, inland areas began to mix more with sleet, while at the coast some areas changed over to plain rain. |Snow and sleet totals ranged from 8 to 13 inches in the Lower Hudson Valley, to 3 to 6 inches in New York City and 2 to 7 inches across Long Island.  At Central Park, 5.5 inches was measured; 4.5 inches at LaGuardia Airport; 2.7 inches at JFK Airport, and 3.3 inches at Islip Airport.|In addition, there were major impacts on mass transit with high winds and heavy wintry precipitation during the evening rush-hour traffic. Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the metropolitan airports with many planes stuck on the tarmac.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 0.50 to 1.00 inches occurred on the night of the 24th as low pressure tracked across central NY. In addition, mild temperatures remained over the region through the end of the month. The mild temperatures continued melting of snowpack. This amounted to a period of prolonged minor flooding at a few river forecast points from the 25th to the 30th. For much of this time, river levels hovered close to or slightly above flood stage along the main branch of the Susquehanna from Waverly east through the Binghamton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air advected into the region on Tuesday March 13th into Wednesday 14th. This led to significant snowmelt across much of east central New York, particularly across the western Mohawk valley and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice began to melt and move along many streams, creeks and rivers during this time.|A slow moving cold front then dropped southeast across the region late Wednesday into Thursday, accompanied by moderate to locally heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, with higher amounts of up to, or slightly in excess of one inch across the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks.|The combination of rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding of small streams and creeks across portions of the east central New York State, starting on March 14th, and lingering until March 18th. In addition, localized river flooding also occurred, especially where ice jams developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed downstream from Schenectady during Thursday afternoon on March 15th, causing the Mohawk River to rise to moderate flood stage Thursday evening. The hardest hit area was within the Stockade neighborhood, particularly North Street and Ingersoll Avenue, where about one dozen people were evacuated by boat, and 30 people in other houses voluntarily evacuated. In addition, power was temporarily turned off to many homes to prevent electrocution hazards.|The river level crested at 226 feet at 5:30 PM EST Thursday evening, and dropped back below the flood stage of 223.0 feet at 8:21 PM EST Thursday evening.

During the early morning hours of the 2nd, the precipitation changed over to freezing rain and continued for several hours before tapering off during the early afternoon of March 2nd. In central NY, up to 1/2 of an inch of ice fell from the southern Tug Hill Plateau, western Mohawk Valley southeast to the western Catskills. The ice caused sporadic power outages and numerous road closures.

A trough of low pressure moved through Long Island the afternoon of March 3rd, bringing scattered showers with it. An apparent dry microburst was associated with the trough's passage at around 3:15 pm in Lindenhurst NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An apparent dry microburst was associated with the trough's passage at around 3:15 pm in Lindenhurst NY, damaging car windows at an auto dealership, and blowing away segments of fencing at the dealership and a near by IHOP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slipped south of the tri-state area during the day on Thursday, March 15.  This allowed colder air to work into the region after several days of anomalously warm temperatures.  Farther to the south, along the Gulf Coast, low pressure developed and deepened as it tracked northeast along the boundary.  The deepening low tracked to near the Mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, March 16, to just east of Montauk Point Saturday morning, March 17. |Initially, the precipitation started off as a light mix of snow, sleet, and rain during the early morning hours, but, as the colder air continued to deepen, the preciptation fell primarily as a mix of snow and sleet during the daytime hours, falling moderate to occasionally heavy at times. The precipitation fell primariliy as snow across the Lower Hudson Valley, while New York City and Long Island were primarily a mix of sleet and snow.  During the evening hours, inland areas began to mix more with sleet, while at the coast some areas changed over to plain rain. |Snow and sleet totals ranged from 8 to 13 inches in the Lower Hudson Valley, to 3 to 6 inches in New York City and 2 to 7 inches across Long Island.  At Central Park, 5.5 inches was measured; 4.5 inches at LaGuardia Airport; 2.7 inches at JFK Airport, and 3.3 inches at Islip Airport.|In addition, there were major impacts on mass transit with high winds and heavy wintry precipitation during the evening rush-hour traffic. Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the metropolitan airports with many planes stuck on the tarmac.

A warm front lifted north through the region during the morning of March 2, followed by a cold front in the afternoon.  This produced widespread heavy rain across Long Island, with storm totals ranging from 1.14 to 2.49 inches.  Widespread flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Roads closed due to flash flooding in Islip.

Morning lows on the 9th were 15 to 30 degrees below zero.|Some morning lows on the 9th included: -29 degrees at Saranac Lake (Franklin), -23 degrees at Tupper Lake (Franklin) and Newcomb (Essex), -20 degrees in Ogdensburg (St. Lawrence), -19 degrees in Chazy (Clinton) and Malone (Franklin), -18 degrees at Massena and Canton (St. Lawrence) and -16 degrees in Plattsburgh (Clinton).

Temperatures across central New York warmed to around 80 degrees ahead of a cold front moving east through the Great Lakes. As the front approached, showers and some thunderstorms developed, and a few of the storms reached severe levels, causing some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator measured a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought 2 to 3 inches of rain across the Catskills before changing over to snow on April 16th. This rainfall on top of existing snow pack was enough to cause minor flooding at a few river forecast points in Binghamton's hydrologic service area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly two inches of rain on top of remaining snow pack caused minor flooding of the Beaver Kill River at Cook's Falls. The river crested at about 11.5 feet at 4:45 am EST. Flood stage is 10 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th.  This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning.  The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning.|As a result of this intense storm, heavy wet snow and sleet fell across much of the southern Adirondacks, Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga Springs region, extending into the eastern Catskills. The snow began Sunday morning, and continued through Sunday night. The heaviest snowfall occurred at higher elevations, especially above 1500 feet.|Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 16 inches, with the greatest amounts across the higher elevations. Some of the highest amounts included 16 inches at Indian Lake, 13.8 inches at Middle Grove, 13.5 inches at Caroga Lake, and 13 inches Rensselaerville.|The heavy wet snowfall, combined with gusty winds, led to numerous downed tree limbs and power lines, which resulted in widespread power outages, particularly across Hamilton county.|The snow eventually mixed with, and changed to rain by Monday morning.

Warm temperatures from April 19th to April 24th led to rapid melting of a deep snowpack across much of the Adirondacks. Antecendent water equivalent in the snow pack ranged from 6 to 16 inches. This rapid melting led to flooding of many rivers that drain the Adirondacks. In addition, releases from Sacandaga Lake perpetuated the flooding on some rivers through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snowmelt led to moderate flooding of West Canada Creek at Hinckley.  The Creek exceeded flood stage of 1227 feet at 0115 EST on April 24th, crested at 1228.11 feet at 0045 EST on April 25th, and fell back below flood stage at 0415 EST on April 26th.

The lake event began late Wednesday April 4th following a sharp cold front.  A massive upper level low then set up over Quebec and poured near record cold across the eastern two thirds of the nation for several days. Late season lake effect snows continued through the 8th. Although Lake Erie had a good deal of ice cover, the moist northwest flow brought residual moisture from the Upper Lakes and dropped up to a foot of upslope and lake enhanced snow across parts of Chautauqua and Cattaraugus counties. |     For the next few days, lake snows became more organized during the late night and early morning periods���breaking up into cellular activity during the afternoon and early evening hours. The heavier accumulations were at night. The Thursday night-Friday morning period brought 6-12��� to much of Oswego county and southern Tug Hill and also another 6 to 12 across Chautauqua county.  The Friday night-Saturday morning period saw an intense band across southern Jefferson county with somewhat of a repeat along the south shore of the Lake Saturday night and Sunday morning. Conditions finally moderated enough to shut down any lake snows late Sunday.|     Some representative total amounts off Lake Erie: Perrysburg 26���; Mayville 23���; Arkwright 18���; and Jamestown 15���.  Off Lake Ontario: Redfield 28���; Parish and Hooker 24���; Highmarket 18���; Fulton 16���; and Constantia 15���.

A mid atmospheric disturbance and surface trough moved into a warm and unstable airmass across northern New York during the afternoon of the 19th. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the mid to late afternoon hours and produced localized wind damage in the form of downed trees and powerlines. Some communities affected included; Malone (Franklin county), Saranac (Clinton county) and Westport (Essex county).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and powerlines downed due to thunderstorm winds in the town of Malone.

A large storm system moved from the Mississippi River valley on the 1st into the Great Lakes on the 2nd, before dissipating north of the Great Lakes on the 3rd. Meanwhile, low pressure developed over the mid-Atlantic coast on the morning of the 2nd, along a boundary that extended from the Great Lakes storm, and moved across eastern New England by nightfall. Snow moved into northern New York around midnight on the 2nd and mixed with sleet and freezing rain before ending by late afternoon.|The combined snow and sleet accumulations across Clinton and Essex counties was 6 to 10 inches that resulted in several vehicle accidents due to the treacherous roads. Some specific accumulations include; 6 inches in AuSable Chasm (Clinton), 7 inches in Plattsburgh (Clinton)and Newcomb (Essex), 8 inches in Moriah (Essex)and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton), 9 inches in Mooers (Clinton) and Crown Point (Essex) and 10 inches in Peru (Clinton).

An arctic cold front swept across northern New York during the afternoon of the 5th and delivered frigid temperatures along with blustery winds. Temperatures plummeted to below zero late on the 5th  and were 10 to 20 degrees below zero by dawn on the 6th. These frigid temperatures, accompanied by winds of 15 to 30 mph created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 degrees below zero. Brisk winds with temperatures around zero continued through the daylight hours of the 6th with wind chill readings in the 20s to around 30 degrees below zero. The winds subsided after sunset on the 6th but it remained extremely cold through the morning of the 7th with overnight minimum temperatures of 15 to 35 degrees below zero.|Some morning lows on the 6th included: -17 degrees at both Saranac Lake and Tupper Lake (Franklin), -16 degrees at Newcomb (Essex) and Malone (Franklin) with -14 degrees at Canton (St. Lawrence) and -12 degrees at Massena (St. Lawrence) and Ellenburg Depot (Clinton).|Some morning lows on the 7th included: -35 degrees in Saranac Lake (Franklin), -31 degrees at Newcomb (Essex), -26 degrees in Ogdensburg (St. Lawrence), -22 degrees in Chazy (Clinton), -21 degrees in Plattsburgh (Clinton), -20 degrees in Canton (St. Lawrence) and -19 degrees in Massena (St. Lawrence) and Malone (Franklin).

Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th.  This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning.  The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning.|As a result of this intense storm, heavy wet snow and sleet fell across much of the southern Adirondacks, Mohawk Valley, the Lake George and Saratoga Springs region, extending into the eastern Catskills. The snow began Sunday morning, and continued through Sunday night. The heaviest snowfall occurred at higher elevations, especially above 1500 feet.|Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 16 inches, with the greatest amounts across the higher elevations. Some of the highest amounts included 16 inches at Indian Lake, 13.8 inches at Middle Grove, 13.5 inches at Caroga Lake, and 13 inches Rensselaerville.|The heavy wet snowfall, combined with gusty winds, led to numerous downed tree limbs and power lines, which resulted in widespread power outages, particularly across Hamilton county.|The snow eventually mixed with, and changed to rain by Monday morning.

Snowfall totals included: Montezuma 12���; Livonia 9���; Adams, Turin, West Monroe and Oswego 7���; and Bristol 15���.

A warming trend started on the 12th, with daytime temperatures in the 40s, that eventually moderated into the 50s by the 14th and 15th. In addition, a quarter to half inch of rain fell along a stationary boundary on the 14th and 15th. The end result was a substantial snow melt that increased surface water runoff into area streams and rivers. These increases caused water levels to rise, which subsequently lifted and moved river ice and created localized ice jams and flooding. |Ice jams occurred along the Great Chazy river at Perrys Mills and Champlain (Clinton county), that resulted in field, road and basement flooding and forced the evacuation of some residents. Another ice jam formed at the confluence of the East and West Branches of the Ausable River in Essex county and resulted in flooding of low-lying roads, fields and some surrounding low-lying buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams occurred at Perrys Mills and Champlain in New York. These jams resulted in field, road and basement flooding and forced evacuations of some residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across central New York warmed to around 80 degrees ahead of a cold front moving east through the Great Lakes. As the front approached, showers and some thunderstorms developed, and a few of the storms reached severe levels, causing some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were knocked down across the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure organized over the southeastern United States during the evening of Saturday April 14th, and tracked northeast along the East Coast Sunday the 15th, to Long Island on Monday the 16th. The low pressure system grew into a major late season winter storm by the time it reached the New Jersey coast Sunday evening the 15th. The central pressure of this storm dropped to 963 mb just south of Long Island on the 16th. The storm stalled in the vicinity of Long Island and the southern New England coast from the 16th until Tuesday the 17th when it finally drifted to well off the east coast.  A mix of light rain and snow overspread central New York during the early morning hours of Sunday April 15th. The precipitation was a mixture of mainly light rain and snow across central New York Sunday morning which transitioned to all rain Sunday afternoon. The precipitation changed back to all snow Sunday night north and west of the Catskills in central New York. The rain turned to all snow in the Catskills later in the morning on Monday the 16th. The snow became heavy at times and continued across central New York until tapering off to lighter snow showers and flurries by the early morning hours on Tuesday the 17th. Total storm accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches in much of southern and western Steuben County and in the lower terrain of Sullivan County, to 4 to 8 inches in the valleys along the southern tier, to between 1 and 2 feet across much of the Finger Lakes region, the higher terrain of central New York, the Catskills and the Tug Hill Plateau. The snow was heavy and wet bringing down many trees and power lines causing scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought between 12 and 18 inches of snow to the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid and unstable airmass, interacted with a lake breeze boundary and led to numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the late afternoon hours of Wednesday June 27th, some of which were severe.||In addition, locally very heavy rain led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a steel pylon at Alcathys Marina, adjacent to where a 17 year old male was standing. The male was knocked unconscious, and was transported to a nearby hospital. He was pronounced dead the next morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from intense thunderstorms led to ponding of water on some streets in Gloversville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were impassable due to 3 or more feet of standing water, or rapidly flowing water of 6 to 12 inches in depth within the city limits of Albany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front which moved through central NY the evening of Friday May 25th. Most of the storms did not produce severe weather. An isolated storm produced large hail in Syracuse NY.

The melting of snow from an above normal snow pack established in February, March and early April, as well as several rain events during the latter half of April, contributed to a significant run-off into streams and rivers of the Lake Champlain basin, which eventually deposited into Lake Champlain. |Lake Champlain had been rising steadily since mid month, and at 7:15 pm LST on April 20th, finally exceeded its flood stage of 100.0 feet. Only minor shoreline flooding resulted in New York. The lake would remain above flood stage through the end of April.
A warm front moved through the region Sunday May 27th touching off scattered showers and thunderstorms. A couple storms produced damaging winds in upstate NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of trees knocked down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 2 particularly potent bow echoes. The first moved through Rockland and Westchester counties, producing extensive damage across south central portions with a microburst associated with a bookend vortex on the left hand side of the bow. The second bow echo moved through Staten Island (Richmond) and Brooklyn (Kings), producing a Gustnado in Brooklyn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm airmass was established across northern New York during the early afternoon hours of the 10th. Meanwhile, a mid-level atmospheric disturbance rotated across the area, and this resulted in the development of thunderstorms. A few of these thunderstorms produced large hail across St. Lawrence county, including penny size (3/4 diameter) hail in Colton and between quarter and golfball size (1.25 diameter) in Brasher Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initiated across the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved across the southern Plains on the 13th into the Gulf coast states on the 14th. On the 14th, this storm intensified rapidly across the southeast United States and continued that trend, as it moved along the Eastern seaboard on the 15th to the western tip of Long Island, NY on the morning of the 16th. Thereafter, this powerful Nor���easter drifted east of New England.|In northern New York, a mixture of snow and rain overspread the area during the late morning of the 15th. Eventually, the precipitation changed to snow as the intensity of the precipitation increased during the afternoon and continued into the early morning hours of the 16th, before gradually tapering off during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 16th.|The heaviest precipitation and largest snowfall totals were across the Adirondacks of Clinton and Essex counties, yet significant wet snowfall occurred within the St. Lawrence River valley due to colder air across the region. Snowfall totals were generally 6 to 9 inches in the valleys with locally more than a foot in the higher elevations of the eastern Adirondacks. This was a heavy, wet snow that caused numerous power outages, as well as extremely slick and treacherous roads that resulted in numerous vehicle accidents and forced road closures. There were two separate single-car crashes on Interstate 87 during the late afternoon and evening of the 15th, which resulted in a total of two fatalities and 6 injuries requiring hospitalization. An area of low pressure organized over the southeastern United States during the evening of Saturday April 14th, and tracked northeast along the East Coast Sunday the 15th, to Long Island on Monday the 16th. The low pressure system grew into a major late season winter storm by the time it reached the New Jersey coast Sunday evening the 15th. The central pressure of this storm dropped to 963 mb just south of Long Island on the 16th. The storm stalled in the vicinity of Long Island and the southern New England coast from the 16th until Tuesday the 17th when it finally drifted to well off the east coast.  A mix of light rain and snow overspread central New York during the early morning hours of Sunday April 15th. The precipitation was a mixture of mainly light rain and snow across central New York Sunday morning which transitioned to all rain Sunday afternoon. The precipitation changed back to all snow Sunday night north and west of the Catskills in central New York. The rain turned to all snow in the Catskills later in the morning on Monday the 16th. The snow became heavy at times and continued across central New York until tapering off to lighter snow showers and flurries by the early morning hours on Tuesday the 17th. Total storm accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches in much of southern and western Steuben County and in the lower terrain of Sullivan County, to 4 to 8 inches in the valleys along the southern tier, to between 1 and 2 feet across much of the Finger Lakes region, the higher terrain of central New York, the Catskills and the Tug Hill Plateau. The snow was heavy and wet bringing down many trees and power lines causing scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought between 4 and 16 inches of snow to the county.

Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 2 particularly potent bow echoes. The first moved through Rockland and Westchester counties, producing extensive damage across south central portions with a microburst associated with a bookend vortex on the left hand side of the bow. The second bow echo moved through Staten Island (Richmond) and Brooklyn (Kings), producing a Gustnado in Brooklyn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid and unstable airmass, interacted with a lake breeze boundary and led to numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the late afternoon hours of Wednesday June 27th, some of which were severe.||In addition, locally very heavy rain led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Green Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 2 particularly potent bow echoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Investigation of the damage by a NWS Meteorologist determined that the damage that occurred was also the result of straight line winds. Estimated wind speeds were at least 70 mph. The most widespread damage observed was from Chappaqua to Bedford Hills to Pound ridge.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing wave of low pressure traveled east along a nearly stationary front during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday May 16th. This triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York State into western New England. Some thunderstorms became locally severe. The low pressure system, and attendant cold front and thunderstorms, moved southeast of the region by Wednesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines near Poughkeepsie. The Red Oaks area was hardest hit, with numerous trees and power lines reported down, with some trees down on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought 2 to 3 inches of rain across the Catskills before changing over to snow on April 16th. This rainfall on top of existing snow pack was enough to cause minor flooding at a few river forecast points in Binghamton's hydrologic service area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt from a major spring storm on April 16th caused the Susquehanna River at Conklin to rise above the 11 foot flood stage. The river crested at 12.4 feet at 9:30 pm on April 19th. At 12.0 feet, flooding begins on Alta, River and Lawrence Roads in the town of Conklin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy rain fell throughout the day and evening of April 15th before changing over to snow during the overnight on the 16th. About 2 inches of rain fell before the change to snow. Snow fell heavy at times during the morning of the 16th, with about 10 inches recorded in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought 2 to 3 inches of rain across the Catskills before changing over to snow on April 16th. This rainfall on top of existing snow pack was enough to cause minor flooding at a few river forecast points in Binghamton's hydrologic service area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 inches of rain falling on existing snow pack brought the Neversink River at Bridgeville over flood stage. The river crested at 15.2 feet on the afternoon of April 16th. Flood stage is 13 feet. At flood stage, minor flooding begins at this location. The river begins to spread into the parking lot of the Holiday Mountain Road ski shop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The melting of snow from an above normal snow pack established in February, March and early April, as well as several rain events during the latter half of April, contributed to a significant run-off into streams and rivers of the Lake Champlain basin, which eventually deposited into Lake Champlain. |Lake Champlain had been rising steadily since mid month, and at 7:15 pm LST on April 20th, finally exceeded its flood stage of 100.0 feet. Only minor shoreline flooding resulted in New York. The lake would remain above flood stage through the end of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm airmass was established across northern New York during the early afternoon hours of the 10th. Meanwhile, a mid-level atmospheric disturbance rotated across the area, and this resulted in the development of thunderstorms. One thunderstorm moved across Massena (St. Lawrence county) during the early to mid-afternoon and produced lightning that struck and burned down a church.

An unseasonably warm airmass was established across northern New York during the early afternoon hours of the 10th. Meanwhile, a mid-level atmospheric disturbance rotated across the area, and this resulted in the development of thunderstorms. A few of these thunderstorms produced large hail across St. Lawrence county, including penny size (3/4 diameter) hail in Colton and between quarter and golfball size (1.25 diameter) in Brasher Falls.

Temperatures across central New York warmed to around 80 degrees ahead of a cold front moving east through the Great Lakes. As the front approached, showers and some thunderstorms developed, and a few of the storms reached severe levels, causing some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofs were blown off at least two houses. There were several reports of damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought between 10 and 15 inches of snow across much of central New York on April 16th. Over the next several days, snow melt from that storm caused minor river flooding at a few of the river points in Binghamton's hydrologic service area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Susquehanna River at Bainbridge rose above the 13 foot flood stage to 13.5 feet at 10:30 pm on April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought between 10 and 15 inches of snow across much of central New York on April 16th. Over the next several days, snow melt from that storm caused minor river flooding at a few of the river points in Binghamton's hydrologic service area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Susquehanna River at Waverly rose above the 11 foot flood stage to 11.4 feet at around 7 am on April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure organized over the southeastern United States during the evening of Saturday April 14th, and tracked northeast along the East Coast Sunday the 15th, to Long Island on Monday the 16th. The low pressure system grew into a major late season winter storm by the time it reached the New Jersey coast Sunday evening the 15th. The central pressure of this storm dropped to 963 mb just south of Long Island on the 16th. The storm stalled in the vicinity of Long Island and the southern New England coast from the 16th until Tuesday the 17th when it finally drifted to well off the east coast.  A mix of light rain and snow overspread central New York during the early morning hours of Sunday April 15th. The precipitation was a mixture of mainly light rain and snow across central New York Sunday morning which transitioned to all rain Sunday afternoon. The precipitation changed back to all snow Sunday night north and west of the Catskills in central New York. The rain turned to all snow in the Catskills later in the morning on Monday the 16th. The snow became heavy at times and continued across central New York until tapering off to lighter snow showers and flurries by the early morning hours on Tuesday the 17th. Total storm accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches in much of southern and western Steuben County and in the lower terrain of Sullivan County, to 4 to 8 inches in the valleys along the southern tier, to between 1 and 2 feet across much of the Finger Lakes region, the higher terrain of central New York, the Catskills and the Tug Hill Plateau. The snow was heavy and wet bringing down many trees and power lines causing scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major spring storm brought from 9 to 14 inches of snow across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast from the Mid Atlantic States across Northeast New Jersey and Eastern Connecticut on the 27th.  A band of heavy rain occurred across Northeast New Jersey northeast across Southern Westchester County, mainly south of I-287, then across Southern Fairfield and New Haven Counties in Connecticut.  Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 3 inches, which resulted in flash flooding across parts of the region.|Storm total rainfall amounts for:|Southern Westchester County - 2.71 inches was measured at Westchester County Airport.|New York City - 2.04 inches was measured at Central Park.|The broadcast media reported flash flooding of the:|Jackie Robinson Parkway west bound at Forest Park in Queens at 10:09 am.|West Side Highway north bound between 79th Street West and 125th Street West in Manhattan at 10:10 am.|Cross County Parkway in both directions at the Bronx River Parkway on ramp and the Hutchinson River Parkway in both directions at Lincoln Avenue in Mount Vernon at 12:24 pm, and the Sprain Brook Parkway south bound after Route 100 in Yonkers at 12:24 pm.

Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th. This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning. The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning.|This intense coastal storm spread heavy precipitation across the lower and mid Hudson Valley region of New York, starting on Sunday, and persisting into late Monday. Initially, the precipitation fell as a mixture of wet snow, sleet and rain, with snow and sleet more prevalent across the higher elevations. The precipitation changed to plain rain by late Sunday afternoon into Monday.  |Liquid equivalent precipitation totals from this storm ranged from 3 to 8 inches.  This led to widespread flooding across the lower and mid Hudson Valley region from late Sunday into Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to flooding of numerous creeks and streams throughout the county.  The Verkeerderkill Creek exceeded bankful around 1655 EST on the 15th, flooding adjacent portions of Ulsterville Road in Walker Valley. Additional flooding was reported around 2313 LST near New Paltz, where several roads were closed due to high water, including Route 299 to Mountain Rest Road, and from Dug Road to Kleinkill Drive.|A state of emergency was declared by 0745 EST on the 16th due to the widespread flooding. Mandatory evacuations also occurred along the Esopus Creek in the town of Ulster around 1430 EST on the 16th.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level atmospheric disturbance and accompanying surface trough moved into a very warm and unstable airmass across Essex county, New York during the afternoon of the 27th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and produced damaging winds in Ticonderoga with numerous reports of trees and power lines down, including trees on two cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees, large limbs and power lines down in Ticonderoga, including trees down on several vehicles on Champlain Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper atmospheric disturbance, along with a weak surface trough moved east across eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 10th. This combination of atmospheric features, passing through during the afternoon hours created numerous showers and thunderstorms, some of which became locally severe, accompanied mainly by large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Cairo from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front traversed eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 31st. This front then gradually lifted to the northeast as a warm front by late Thursday afternoon and evening.||This front, combined with several weak upper level atmospheric disturbances passing through, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region, some of which became locally severe Thursday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down from thunderstorm winds in West Sand Lake.||In addition, a police officer and firefighter were both injured in North Greenbush while rescuing 2 people from an auto accident during a thunderstorm, as a large limb from a maple tree fell on them due to strong winds. The police officer was hit on the head, and stayed in the hospital overnight for observation, while the firefighter was hit on the arm and stomach, and was treated and released from the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air advected into the region on Tuesday March 13th into Wednesday 14th. This led to significant snowmelt across much of east central New York, particularly across the western Mohawk valley and southern Adirondacks. In addition, ice began to melt and move along many streams, creeks and rivers during this time.|A slow moving cold front then dropped southeast across the region late Wednesday into Thursday, accompanied by moderate to locally heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts generally ranged from one quarter to one half of an inch, with higher amounts of up to, or slightly in excess of one inch across the western Mohawk Valley and southern Adirondacks.|The combination of rainfall, snowmelt and ice jams led to flooding of small streams and creeks across portions of the east central New York State, starting on March 14th, and lingering until March 18th. In addition, localized river flooding also occurred, especially where ice jams developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snowmelt, runoff from moderate rainfall, and ice jams on the Mohawk River led to flooding in portions of Montgomery county during the afternoon and evening hours of Thursday March 15th.|Flooding was reported in Fort Plain, at a Curtis Lumber Store, and also along the Canajoharie Creek near Marshville due to local runoff.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed east across east central New York and western New England during the afternoon hours of Tuesday June 5th. The interaction of this strong front, combined with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two females, ages 18 and 16, were standing under a tree in Schenectady when lightning struck the tree, injuring both females. The 18 year old female was airlifted to Albany Medical Center for burns to her feet, ankles, legs, stomach, and chest, and may have also had internal injuries, while the 16 year old female suffered superficial wounds to her right arm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. An isolated storm reached severe limits and dropped 3/4 hail in the Town of Brant.

A cold front slowly settled south across the Adirondacks of east central New York State during the afternoon hours of Saturday June 2nd. The front, combined with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of scattered thunderstorms Saturday afternoon and evening, some of which became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in the Warner Bay portion of Lake George, near Cleverdale.

Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong cold front as it approached central New York and Northeast Pennsylvania. Widespread severe thunderstorms developed, with many reports of large hail and damaging winds. One thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in the eastern Finger Lakes region.

Hot and humid weather developed as a Bermuda high pressure system built into the Northeast United States. A cold front moved into the Great Lakes during the day on the 27th, with thunderstorms developing in the favorable environment across the area. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 27th, with some of the storms producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved off the east coast ahead of an approaching cold front, allowing southwest winds to bring a flow of hot and humid air across the region. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of this strong cold front, with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 206/County Route 7 between Downsville and Roscoe was closed due to flash flooding. Heavy rain began significant flooding of this area at 730 am EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure moved off the east coast ahead of an approaching cold front, allowing southwest winds to bring a flow of hot and humid air across the region. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of this strong cold front, with numerous reports of large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A catastrophic flash flood occurred the evening of June 19th. The area along the border of Delaware and Sullivan Counties received between 8 and 11 inches of rain in 2 to 3 hours. The water washed out one bridge, damaged several other bridges and roads, destroyed all land-line and cellular communications and ripped homes from their foundations.|4 people were killed by what was described as a 4 to 8 foot wall of water which torn down the hill close to route 206/county route 7. 3 of the people that were killed were in homes that were washed away. 1 person was in a car. The flooding abated late in the evening.  ||A detailed assessment of the damages to the homes and businesses in Delaware county was conducted by the New York State Emergency Management Office and the Federal Emergency Management Agency. These assessments found that 37 homes had been destroyed and 16 additional homes had suffered major damages. A section of route 206/county route 7 was destroyed between Rockland and Downsville. The flooding tore a 26 foot deep gully into state route 206/county route 7. Total damages were around 30 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season Nor'easter impacted the region with a prolonged period of moderate coastal flooding.  The low orignated over the southern plains on Friday, April 13, and then reached the mid Atlantic coast Sunday morning, April 15.  From there, it underwent rapid deepening and tracked northeast, reaching the waters off southern New England Sunday night.  The combination of a strong high off the New England coast and a period of higher than normal spring tides resulted in several days of tidal piling across Atlantic facing beaches and Long Island Sound. The slow eastward movement of the low in the following days resulted in a prolonged period of long fetch easterly flow.||Tidal departures were highest on both the Atlantic back bays of western Long Island and western Long Island Sound from Sunday evening through Monday morning, ranging from 2.5 to 3.5 feet. Thereafter, there were fewer, more isolated flooding episodes through Thursday, April 19.||The moderarte coastal flooding at Kings Point, New York, resulted in a tide level of 11.57 feet (MLLW) at 10:36 p.m. on Sunday, April 15. This was comparable to tide levels during Hurricanes Gloria and Donna.||The storm caused over $26 million in damage in Suffolk County, including significant beach erosion, flooding, and harm to homes, businesses, and infrastructure.||Several days prior to this event, model storm surge forecasts were as high as 4.5 feet.  For Atlantic facing shores, this would have been comparable to the October 1996 and the Halloween 1991 Nor'easters. For Long Island Sound, such a surge would have produced flooding approaching the level of the December 1992 Nor'easter. While these levels were not realized due to changes in the forecast track, this storm resulted in considerable damage to property with estimates into the millions of dollars.

A strong low pressure system tracked northeast across the Great Lakes into Hudson Bay. A strong southwest flow developed ahead of the associated cold front and brought a warm and humid airmass across New York and Pennsylvania in advance of the front. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front...producing hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down by strong thunderstorm winds.

A warm, very moist and unstable airmass, combined with the approach of a weak cold front from the west, led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms during the mid afternoon hours on Friday July 27th.||Isolated severe thunderstorms occurred in portions of the western Mohawk Valley. In addition, with abundant moisture in place, and repeated clusters of thunderstorms forming over the same region, localized flash flooding also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from several clusters of thunderstorms led to minor flooding in portions of Gloversville. Several roads were closed, including Second Avenue, outside of the Boulevard Elementary School, due to approximately 2 feet of standing water.||In addition, the Boulevard Elementary School sustained water damage as high water levels led to water infiltration through temporary windows, which were not completely sealed. This allowed approximately 1 foot of water to enter the basement, damaging water heaters and other stored items such as textbooks.
